Narrative Classification of Crypto-Exposed Firms

This document reports the results of a systematic narrative classification of the crypt-exposed firms. Each firm is assigned fractional weights across six transmission mechanism categories -- crypto holders, miners, financial services, non-financial services, infrastructure providers, and unclassified -- based on evidence retrieved from earnings call transcripts, SEC filings, and financial news covering January 2017 to December 2024. Classification is performed by a large language model (GPT-5) instructed via a structured prompt (firm_instructions.md) and executed through teh GPT API using the pipeline script GO_firm-GPT.py. The full classification methodology, category definitions, weighting rules, and output schema are documented in the accompanying instructions file.

Executed: 2026-03-13 14:12 UTC | Model: GPT-5.4

HIVE DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES

  • Rank: 1 | Expo: 19.97 | id_str: CA4339211035
  • Weight summary: holders 0.2, miners 0.8, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 4 | AssetShare: 3
  • Primary sources: earnings releases / call materials (2022–2024), quarterly and annual MD&A / filings (2024), company investor materials

Firm Overview

HIVE Digital Technologies Ltd., formerly HIVE Blockchain Technologies Ltd., is a Canadian-listed digital asset mining company that operates renewable-energy-focused Bitcoin mining data centers and, more recently, a smaller HPC/AI compute business. Across 2017–2024 sources, crypto exposure is overwhelmingly tied to Bitcoin mining economics, with balance-sheet Bitcoin primarily arising from mined coin rather than a separate treasury-investment model.

Exposure Channels Identified

miners is the dominant channel because management repeatedly describes HIVE as a digital asset miner, discloses Bitcoin production, hashrate growth, mining margins, and mining revenue, and frames profitability around network difficulty, halving effects, and energy costs. In Q2 FY2024 results, management said the firm “produced 801 Bitcoin this quarter,” and discussed mining equipment ROI and hashrate growth. Source name

A smaller holders weight is warranted because HIVE also explicitly emphasizes a Bitcoin “HODL” posture and reports large unencumbered Bitcoin balances on its balance sheet. FY2024 filings reported 2,287 Bitcoin on balance sheet worth $161.3 million and referred to strategic investments including “building BTC assets on our balance sheet,” which goes somewhat beyond merely holding coin briefly before sale. Source name

Weight Assignment

The 0.8 / 0.2 split reflects that mining is the core economic mechanism by a wide margin: FY2024 digital currency mining revenue was $111.0 million versus only $3.4 million from HPC, and company disclosures consistently center production, hashrate, miner purchases, difficulty, and halving impacts. At the same time, the evidence supports a non-zero holders share because management repeatedly markets the Bitcoin balance as a HODL / asset-building feature, not just operational inventory. No finserv, bizserv, or infrastr weight is assigned because HIVE is not evidenced here as a crypto exchange/custodian, non-financial service provider to third parties, or a picks-and-shovels infrastructure vendor; it is principally a self-operated miner.

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main boundary issue is whether HIVE’s Bitcoin balance should be treated purely as mined inventory (miners) or partly as deliberate treasury exposure (holders). The sources support a modest holders allocation because management uses explicit HODL language and cites BTC balance-building as a use of proceeds, but most of those holdings still originate from mining rewards, so miners remains dominant. Direct third-party transcript pages were partly blocked, so the report relies on company earnings releases / webcast materials that reproduce management remarks and on filed MD&A / exhibit documents.

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Aug. 2022, company earnings release / call materials: “HIVE was dedicated to navigating through this crypto storm for our shareholders” and referred to mark-to-market changes in its “investment portfolio in digital assets.” — Source name
  • Nov. 2023, company earnings release / call materials: “We produced 801 Bitcoin this quarter” and discussed mining difficulty, ASIC ROI, and gross mining margin. — Source name
  • June 2024, Q4 FY2024 webcast deck: slide titled “Bitcoin On The Balance Sheet / HIVE’s HODL” shows 2,287 BTC at Mar. 2024 and 2,451 BTC at May 2024. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • June 24, 2024, filed results / annual disclosure: FY2024 revenue included “Revenue from digital currency mining was $111.0 million” and the company “mined 3,123 Bitcoin”. — Source name
  • June 24, 2024, same filing: HIVE ended FY2024 with “2,287 Bitcoin on the balance sheet as at March 31, 2024 worth $161.3 million.” — Source name
  • June 24, 2024, same filing: use of proceeds included “strategic investments including building BTC assets on our balance sheet.” — Source name
  • Sept. 2024 quarter filing / MD&A: “The Company currently holds Bitcoin primarily” and other GPU-mined coins were “regularly exchanged for Bitcoin.” — Source name
  • Dec. 2024 MD&A: after the 2023 name change, HIVE said it would “maintain a strong presence in Bitcoin mining” while diversifying into AI/HPC. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Apr. 2024, company production update: HIVE explicitly described “HODL Growth in March 2024 to 2,287 Bitcoins.” — Source name
  • Sept. 2024 MD&A risk disclosure: management states “the value of our stock and our Bitcoin assets may be subject to greater volatility stemming from industry developments not directly related to our mining business,” supporting invbase = 1 as a crypto-proxy equity. — Source name
  • June 2025 filed FY2025 release, used only as corroborative context beyond the sample: HIVE still reported most revenue from digital currency mining and described mined Bitcoin as contributing to its HODL position. — Source name

BAKKT HOLDINGS A

  • Rank: 2 | Expo: 18.61 | id_str: US05759B3050
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 1.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 5 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: 2023–2024 earnings call transcripts, 2023–2024 10-K filings, company press releases / financial news

Firm Overview

Bakkt Holdings, Inc. is a U.S.-listed financial technology company whose crypto activities sit within a digital asset trading, brokerage, custody, and embedded crypto-services platform rather than a mining or treasury-holding model. Across 2023–2024 sources, the company describes itself as providing crypto trading and custody solutions to clients and their end customers, with crypto services becoming the dominant revenue driver after the April 1, 2023 acquisition of Apex Crypto. (sec.gov)

Exposure Channels Identified

finserv is the clear and exclusive mechanism. Bakkt’s filings state that its platform lets customers “buy, sell and store crypto” through APIs or embedded web experiences, and that it offers trading plus custody services for retail customers through client platforms. (sec.gov)

The transcript evidence is consistent with that classification. In the Q2 2023 earnings call, management said, “Gross crypto services revenue of $335.3 million,” discussed crypto trading activity, transacting crypto accounts, notional traded volume, and assets under custody, and answered analyst questions about Bakkt’s custody platform and Bitcoin ETF custody opportunities. (insidermonkey.com)

No holders weight is assigned because the evidence here is about customer-facing trading/custody operations, not deliberate treasury accumulation. No miners, bizserv, or infrastr weight is assigned because Bakkt is not evidenced in the sample period as a miner, a non-financial service provider to crypto firms, or a picks-and-shovels infrastructure vendor; rather, it directly intermediates and custodies digital assets. (sec.gov)

Weight Assignment

A full 1.0 weight to finserv is warranted because the firm’s crypto exposure is overwhelmingly tied to exchange/brokerage-like trading, custody, and embedded crypto financial services. The 2024 Form 10-K shows crypto services revenue of $3.441 billion versus $49.2 million of loyalty services revenue, meaning crypto-related financial services overwhelmingly dominated total revenue by 2024; meanwhile, assets under custody were reported as customer assets rather than a proprietary crypto treasury. This leaves no basis in the collected evidence for splitting weight across other mechanisms. (sec.gov)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main boundary issue is whether Bakkt should receive any infrastr weight because management sometimes uses “platform” and “infrastructure” language. Under the project rules, however, the firm belongs in finserv because the evidence shows it directly handles crypto trading, brokerage flow, custody, and customer assets rather than merely supplying neutral back-end tools. invbase = 1 is supported, but more by market characterization than by management explicitly describing a crypto-focused shareholder base: contemporaneous news described Bakkt as a crypto-focused company whose shares moved sharply with crypto-related developments, and the company’s business concentration in crypto services makes it a recognizable crypto proxy equity. (coindesk.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Aug. 2023, Q2 2023 earnings call transcript: “Gross crypto services revenue of $335.3 million” and management discussed crypto trading volume, crypto transacting accounts, and assets under custody. — Source name
  • Aug. 2023, same transcript: management said “Custody has always been a very important part of our business” and described client interest in its “institutional-grade custody platform.” — Source name
  • Nov. 2024, Q3 2024 earnings call transcript summary: “Gross crypto services revenue for the quarter was $316.3 million” and assets under custody at custodian partners were $938.7 million. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Mar. 2025, FY2024 Form 10-K: Bakkt says its platform enables customers to “buy, sell and store crypto” via APIs or embedded web experience. — Source name
  • Mar. 2025, FY2024 Form 10-K: Bakkt states it offered an institutional-grade qualified custody solution through Bakkt Trust and also uses BitGo and Coinbase Custody relationships for Bakkt Crypto. — Source name
  • Mar. 2025, FY2024 Form 10-K: crypto services revenue was $3,441.1 million in 2024 versus $727.0 million in 2023; total revenue was $3,490.2 million. — Source name
  • Mar. 2025, FY2024 Form 10-K: assets under custody were $2,301.9 million as of December 31, 2024. — Source name
  • Apr. 2023, acquisition press release: Apex Crypto provided execution, clearing, custody, cost basis and tax services, and Bakkt said the acquisition added immediate crypto scale. — Source name
  • Nov. 2023, custody product release: Bakkt announced relaunch of Bakkt Custody with expanded asset support and framed qualified custody as a core need in crypto markets. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Oct. 2021, CoinDesk: Bakkt shares surged after crypto-payment partnerships, reflecting investor treatment of the stock as a crypto-linked equity. — Source name
  • Nov. 18, 2024, CNBC/Reuters: report on Trump Media talks to buy Bakkt said the company was founded to offer tech services for crypto investors, and the news sent shares sharply higher. — Source name
  • Mar. 2025, FY2024 Form 10-K: approximately 74% of Bakkt’s crypto services revenue came from a single client, Webull, highlighting that the crypto business is economically central rather than incidental. — Source name

COINBASE GLOBAL A

  • Rank: 3 | Expo: 18.55 | id_str: US19260Q1076
  • Weight summary: holders 0.1, miners 0.0, finserv 0.9, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 5 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: 2024 Form 10-K, Q4 2023/Q1 2023/Q2 2024 earnings call transcripts, financial news on Coinbase as a crypto-linked stock

Firm Overview

Coinbase Global, Inc. is a U.S.-listed crypto platform whose core businesses during 2017–2024 were retail and institutional crypto trading, custody, staking, stablecoin-related revenue, and related onchain financial products. Within the sample period, its crypto exposure is overwhelmingly direct and financial in nature, while it also maintained a smaller proprietary portfolio of crypto assets held for investment. (sec.gov)

Exposure Channels Identified

finserv is the dominant channel. Management repeatedly discussed trading volume, transaction revenue, custody, staking, Prime brokerage activity, derivatives, and stablecoin activity, all of which fit the project’s crypto financial services category. In Q1 2023, Coinbase said it generated revenue growth “across the board,” with transaction revenues of $375 million and subscription-and-services revenue of $362 million, including growth “particularly across staking and custody.” In Q2 2024, management highlighted Base, smart wallets, and nearly $20 billion per week in USDC transaction volume on Base, but these products were still framed as part of Coinbase’s crypto platform and revenue model rather than neutral infrastructure sold to miners or third parties. (fool.com)

A smaller holders weight is also warranted because the 2024 Form 10-K explicitly states that Coinbase held $1.6 billion of crypto assets “for investment,” viewed these as “primarily long-term holdings,” and did “not plan to engage in regular trading” of them. That is deliberate treasury-style ownership separate from customer custody and therefore fits the project’s holders definition, but it is secondary relative to the scale of exchange, custody, and transaction-based operations. No miners, bizserv, or infrastr weight is assigned because the retrieved evidence does not show Coinbase operating mining assets, providing non-financial support services as its main crypto mechanism, or selling mining/compute/energy infrastructure. (sec.gov)

Weight Assignment

A finserv weight of 0.9 and holders weight of 0.1 best reflects the evidence. Coinbase’s 2024 Form 10-K reports $3.986 billion of transaction revenue and $2.307 billion of subscription and services revenue, including stablecoin, blockchain rewards, and custodial fee revenue, showing that crypto financial intermediation overwhelmingly drives the business. The same filing also shows $1.553 billion of crypto assets held for investment at year-end 2024, which is meaningful enough to justify a non-zero holders share, but still small relative to a business with roughly $6.56 billion of total revenue and extensive customer-facing trading/custody operations. (sec.gov)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main classification boundary is whether some of Coinbase’s developer and onchain products, especially Base and Coinbase Developer Platform, justify any infrastr weight. Under the project rules, they do not dominate the classification here because management and filings tie them back to Coinbase’s broader crypto financial platform, transaction flows, stablecoin usage, and customer monetization rather than to purpose-built mining/datacenter infrastructure. invbase = 1 is appropriate because market coverage repeatedly treated Coinbase shares as a crypto-linked equity that moved with bitcoin and broader crypto sentiment. None identified beyond that boundary issue. (fool.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • May 4, 2023, Q1 2023 earnings call transcript: “Transaction revenues increased 16% quarter over quarter to $375 million” and CFO said growth was seen “particularly across staking and custody.” — Source name
  • Feb. 15, 2024, Q4 2023 earnings call transcript: “We saw inflows in custody and increase in stakes balances” and Coinbase closed 2023 with “just under $200 billion of assets on platform.” — Source name
  • Aug. 1, 2024, Q2 2024 earnings call transcript: “Subscription and services revenue reached an all-time high” and management said Coinbase was seeing almost “$20 billion per week in USDC transaction volume on Base.” — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Feb. 2025, FY2024 Form 10-K: Coinbase reported total 2024 transaction revenue of $3.986 billion. — Source name
  • Feb. 2025, FY2024 Form 10-K: subscription and services revenue was $2.307 billion in 2024, including $910.5 million stablecoin revenue, $705.8 million blockchain rewards, and $141.7 million custodial fee revenue. — Source name
  • Feb. 2025, FY2024 Form 10-K: Coinbase stated, “we held the following crypto assets: $1.6 billion held for investment” as of Dec. 31, 2024. — Source name
  • Feb. 2025, FY2024 Form 10-K: Coinbase said it viewed its crypto asset investments as “primarily long-term holdings” and did not plan “to engage in regular trading” of them. — Source name
  • Feb. 2025, FY2024 Form 10-K: the filing retrospectively adjusted away previously reported 2023 safeguarding customer crypto assets of $192.6 billion under SAB 122, indicating those were customer-related custody balances rather than proprietary treasury assets. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Apr. 13, 2021, Reuters: bitcoin hit a record before Coinbase’s listing, underscoring the company’s role as a public-market crypto bellwether. — Source name
  • July 15, 2024, CNBC: “Crypto stocks like Coinbase and MicroStrategy soar as bitcoin rallies,” showing equity-market treatment of Coinbase as a crypto proxy. — Source name
  • Nov. 6, 2024, AP: Coinbase shares jumped 17% as bitcoin hit a record after the U.S. election, further evidencing investor-base spillovers from crypto sentiment. — Source name

DEFI TECHNOLOGIES

  • Rank: 4 | Expo: 18.19 | id_str: CA2449161025
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 0.9, bizserv 0.1, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 4 | AssetShare: 4
  • Primary sources: 2024 financial statements & MD&A, 2024 Annual Information Form, May 2024 earnings call transcript listing

Firm Overview

DeFi Technologies Inc. is a Canadian-listed digital-asset company whose main crypto businesses during the sample period were its Valour exchange-traded product platform, Stillman Digital OTC/liquidity operations, DeFi Alpha trading activities, and Reflexivity Research. Retrieved sources show that crypto exposure sits at the center of the firm’s business model rather than at the periphery.

Exposure Channels Identified

The dominant channel is finserv. The company explicitly operates digital-asset ETPs through Valour, earns management fees on those ETPs, engages in staking and lending tied to digital assets, and owns Stillman Digital, a non-custodial spot digital asset OTC desk and liquidity provider. A 2024 transcript record is retrievable from Seeking Alpha’s DeFi Technologies earnings transcript page, anchoring that public earnings-call coverage existed in the sample period, while filings provide the substantive detail on the operating model.

A smaller bizserv weight is warranted because DeFi Technologies also owns Reflexivity Research, which produces crypto-native research reports and organizes investor conferences for the crypto industry. That activity is clearly crypto-related but is non-financial support/service activity and is secondary to the company’s much larger ETP, trading, staking, lending, and liquidity businesses. No holders weight is assigned because the large digital-asset balances in the statements are tied mainly to ETP hedging/redemption structures and related financial operations rather than a pure treasury HODL policy; no evidence of mining or crypto infrastructure supply was retrieved.

Weight Assignment

A finserv weight of 0.9 and bizserv weight of 0.1 best fits the evidence. The 2024 AIF states revenue and cash flow are generated from disposition of investments, ETP management fees, and staking/lending activities, while the 2024 financial statements report C$489.9 million of digital assets against C$508.1 million of ETP holders payable at December 31, 2024, indicating that balance-sheet crypto exposure is overwhelmingly tied to financial-product issuance and related trading/hedging mechanics rather than standalone treasury investing. Reflexivity Research is meaningful but still a minority line of business relative to the scale and centrality of the firm’s digital-asset financial services operations.

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main boundary issue is whether some of the company’s digital assets should generate a non-zero holders weight. The exclusion rule points against that here because filings tie much of the asset base to ETP liabilities, lending, staking, hedging, and trading activity rather than to a separate treasury accumulation strategy. The retrieved earnings-call evidence is limited because the full transcript page itself was discoverable but not fully fetchable due to access restrictions; accordingly, the call anchor is weaker than the filing evidence and certainty is kept at 3 rather than 4.

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • May 21, 2024, earnings call transcript listing: Seeking Alpha’s DeFi Technologies transcript page shows a retrievable 2024 earnings call transcript entry dated May 21, 2024, confirming earnings-call coverage in the sample period. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Mar. 30, 2025, Annual Information Form for FY2024: the company states it operates five lines of business: “Asset Management, Ventures, DeFi Alpha, Reflexivity Research and Stillman Digital.” — Source name
  • Mar. 30, 2025, Annual Information Form for FY2024: revenue and cash flow are generated from “management fees of ETPs and staking and lending activities of cryptocurrencies and DeFi protocol tokens.” — Source name
  • Mar. 30, 2025, Annual Information Form for FY2024: Stillman Digital is described as “a non-custodial, spot digital asset OTC desk and digital asset liquidity provider” with block trading and market-making services. — Source name
  • Mar. 30, 2025, Annual Information Form for FY2024: Reflexivity Research “produces commissioned reports and organizes thematic conferences” in the cryptocurrency sector. — Source name
  • 2024 financial statements & MD&A: revenue recognition notes say the company recognizes revenue from “management fees earned on various ETP products,” “Research revenues,” and “Validator Node revenue.” — Source name
  • 2024 financial statements & MD&A: at Dec. 31, 2024, DeFi Technologies reported digital assets of C$489,865,638 and “ETP holders payable” of C$508,130,490. — Source name
  • 2024 interim financial statements: the note on ETPs lists numerous Valour crypto ETPs and their fair values, evidencing direct operation of exchange-traded digital-asset products. — Source name
  • 2024 40-F annual report: the company states, “We buy and sell digital assets primarily on non-U.S. exchanges,” consistent with direct crypto financial activity. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • June 20, 2024, CoinDesk: the company was described as a “cryptocurrency exchange-traded product issuer,” reinforcing market characterization of its main exposure as crypto financial services. — Source name
  • Mar. 30, 2025, Annual Information Form for FY2024: the company states that because Valour’s ETPs track digital-asset prices, “the value of the Common Shares relates partially to the value of such digital assets,” supporting invbase = 1. — Source name

OLB GROUP

  • Rank: 5 | Expo: 17.32 | id_str: US67086U4067
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.9, finserv 0.1, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: 2022–2024 SEC filings, DMINT 2024 S-1, Seeking Alpha earnings transcript listings

Firm Overview

The OLB Group, Inc. is a U.S.-listed fintech and merchant-services company whose core business is payment processing and e-commerce services. Within the 2017–2024 sample period, its crypto exposure came primarily through its DMINT subsidiary’s Bitcoin mining operations, with a smaller adjacent crypto-financial angle through OLBit’s planned money-transmission and BitLicense applications. (sec.gov)

Exposure Channels Identified

miners is the clear primary category. OLB’s filings state that DMINT was formed in July 2021 “to operate in the cryptocurrency mining industry,” that it deployed ASIC Antminer S19J Pro machines configured to mine Bitcoin, and that as of December 31, 2022 it had 1,000 computers online and mining, later relocated to Selmer, Tennessee. (sec.gov)

A small finserv weight is also warranted because OLB formed OLBit in May 2021 to pursue an “emerging money transmission and transactional business,” and the company disclosed that OLBit was applying for state money-transmission licenses and a New York BitLicense, although management also said this effort was paused to focus on payments and Bitcoin mining. That makes the financial-services channel real but still clearly secondary and largely developmental rather than economically central during the sample period. (sec.gov)

Weight Assignment

A miners weight of 0.9 and finserv weight of 0.1 best fits the evidence. The mining business is repeatedly and explicitly documented in OLB’s filings, operating descriptions, depreciation disclosures, bitcoin sale disclosures, and the DMINT spin-off registration materials, while the OLBit activity appears more preparatory than revenue-bearing. No holders weight is assigned because the bitcoin on hand appears incidental to mining operations and was mostly sold down rather than described as a deliberate treasury strategy; no evidence supports bizserv or crypto-focused infrastr exposure beyond OLB’s own mining operations. (sec.gov)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main ambiguity is whether OLBit merits any non-zero finserv weight, because filings describe licensing efforts but do not show material crypto transaction revenue from that subsidiary in the retrieved evidence. A small 0.1 weight is still appropriate because management explicitly positioned OLBit around money transmission and crypto-related transactional activity, but the evidence suggests it remained secondary to mining. The earnings-call anchor is weaker than ideal because the full transcript text was not fetchable; however, Seeking Alpha’s transcript page does show OLB earnings-call coverage in the sample period, satisfying the call-anchor requirement at a moderate-confidence level. (seekingalpha.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • May 13, 2022, Seeking Alpha transcript listing: Seeking Alpha’s OLB earnings transcript page shows a retrievable transcript entry dated May 13, 2022, confirming public earnings-call coverage during the sample period. — Source name
  • Mar. 28, 2022, Seeking Alpha transcript listing: the same transcript page shows another OLB earnings-call transcript entry dated March 28, 2022, reinforcing that earnings-call materials existed for the company. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Apr. 1, 2023, Form 10-K for FY2022: OLB states, “On July 23, 2021, we formed DMINT, Inc. … to operate in the cryptocurrency mining industry,” and notes that “as of December 31, 2022, DMINT had 1,000 computers online and mining for Bitcoin.” — Source name
  • Apr. 1, 2023, Form 10-K for FY2022: the company says OLBit was formed “to hold the Company’s assets and operate its business related to its emerging money transmission and transactional business,” and was “in the process of applying for money transmission licenses in all 50 states along with New York Bitlicense.” — Source name
  • Apr. 1, 2024, Form 10-K for FY2023: OLB reports, “Depreciation expense for our Bitcoin Mining Segment was $2,560,015” in 2023 and also recognized “a realized gain from the sale of bitcoin of $288,584.” — Source name
  • Apr. 1, 2024, Form 10-K for FY2023: management states it “paused any non-essential spending on legal and consulting advisors in connection with OLBit’s State Money Transmission License and New York BitLicense applications to focus on the Company’s payment processing business and Bitcoin mining business.” — Source name
  • Oct. 21, 2024, DMINT Form S-1: DMINT is described as “a Bitcoin mining company” and the filing says the Bradford, Pennsylvania facility “was … used … for bitcoin mining operations through March of 2023.” — Source name
  • Oct. 21, 2024, DMINT Form S-1: the filing reports that as of June 30, 2024 DMINT had only 0.001 bitcoin on hand, versus 11.14 bitcoin at December 31, 2023, supporting the view that coin balances were incidental to mining rather than a treasury HODL strategy. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Oct. 19, 2021, CoinDesk: OLB was reported to have started Bitcoin mining with 100 Antminer S19j Pro machines, and management outlined plans for much larger mining capacity, corroborating the market’s view of the firm as a crypto miner. — Source name
  • Mar. 29, 2022, Business Wire: OLB described itself as “a diversified Fintech eCommerce merchant services provider and Bitcoin cryptocurrency mining enterprise,” and disclosed 2021 cryptocurrency-business revenue of $304,004. — Source name

DMG BLOCKCHAIN SOLUTIONS

  • Rank: 6 | Expo: 16.55 | id_str: CA23345B2003
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.8, finserv 0.2, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 4 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: company financial-results pages and transcript listings (2023–2024), company press releases (2024), quarterly/full-year results disclosures (2023–2024)

Firm Overview

DMG Blockchain Solutions Inc. is a Canadian publicly listed blockchain company focused primarily on Bitcoin self-mining, while also developing blockchain software, a mining pool, and, by 2024, an institutional digital-asset custody subsidiary. Across the 2017–2024 sample period, the retrieved evidence shows that mining remained the core economic activity, with financial-services activity emerging but still secondary by the end of 2024.

Exposure Channels Identified

The dominant exposure channel is miners. DMG repeatedly describes mined bitcoin volumes, mining revenue, hashrate expansion, mining equipment purchases, and mining-focused operating results, and its 2023 and 2024 results releases explicitly tie revenue performance to digital currency mining. The transcript anchor is also available through the company’s investor page, which lists multiple Seeking Alpha transcript links for 2024 and 2025 and confirms conference calls tied to those results releases.

A smaller finserv weight is also warranted. DMG’s 2024 disclosures describe Terra Pool, Blockseer/Core+, and especially Systemic Trust, an Alberta digital-asset trust company intended to provide institutional custody and related compliant transaction services. That said, the retrieved evidence does not show these custody/trust activities contributing material revenue during 2024, so they are real but still clearly secondary to mining.

Weight Assignment

A miners weight of 0.8 and finserv weight of 0.2 best fits the evidence. Mining is the firm’s repeatedly documented revenue engine and operating focus, with 2023 revenue of C$27.9 million alongside 878 bitcoin mined, and 2024 results again attributing growth to digital-currency mining revenue. A non-zero finserv weight is justified because by August 2024 DMG had formally incorporated Systemic Trust to pursue institutional digital-asset custody, and management also highlighted Terra Pool and transaction-monetization efforts; however, these activities appear developmental relative to the mining business. No holders weight is assigned because the company’s bitcoin balances appear mainly connected to mined inventory and revaluation rather than a clearly articulated treasury-accumulation strategy during the sample period. invbase = 1 is appropriate because financial media explicitly characterized DMG as a crypto miner, making it a market crypto proxy.

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main ambiguity is whether DMG’s software/custody activities should receive more than a modest finserv weight. The company clearly promoted custody and transaction-related offerings in 2024, but the retrieved sources do not show them overtaking or even approaching mining in economic importance by December 2024. A second limitation is that the transcript pages themselves were not directly fetchable due access restrictions, so the call anchor relies on the company investor page’s transcript listings and the associated result-release pages announcing conference calls; this supports a confident but not maximum certainty score. None of the retrieved 2017–2024 evidence supports a separate holders classification beyond operationally held mined bitcoin.

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Dec. 21, 2023, company financials page / transcript listing: DMG’s investor page lists “Q4 | 2024 Final | Transcript” and earlier transcript links, showing recurring earnings-call transcript availability for the firm. — Source name
  • Feb. 22, 2024, Q1 2024 results release: the company announced it “will host a conference call to review first quarter 2024 financial results and provide a corporate update” on February 22, 2024. — Source name
  • Dec. 21, 2023, Q4 2023 results release: the company announced it would host a conference call to review fourth-quarter 2023 financial results and provide a corporate update. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Dec. 21, 2023, FY2023 results release: DMG reported “$27.9 million revenue,” “878 bitcoin in 2023,” and year-end “$18.9 million in cash and digital currency” with “$82.6M of total assets.” — Source name
  • Feb. 22, 2024, Q1 2024 results release: management said results benefited from “45% higher hashrate” and a “30% increase in the quarterly average price of bitcoin,” while continuing to invest in “Bitcoin network software infrastructure and applications.” — Source name
  • Aug. 27, 2024, Q3 2024 results release: DMG said the increase in digital currency mining revenue reflected a 139% increase in the average bitcoin price, and highlighted a balance sheet with “$39.6 million in cash and digital currency” and “$111 million in total assets.” — Source name
  • Dec. 19, 2024, FY2024 results release: the company said revenue growth was attributable to “increases in digital currency mining revenues,” alongside unrealized and realized gains on digital currency. — Source name
  • Aug. 7, 2024, press release: DMG announced incorporation of Systemic Trust Company to pursue institutional digital-asset custody for clients including trading platforms, banks, asset managers, corporations, and government agencies. — Source name
  • Sep. 12, 2024, press release: DMG said Systemic Trust had completed SOC 2 attestation, reinforcing that custody was a real operating initiative by late 2024. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Nov. 30, 2021, CoinDesk: DMG was described as a “crypto miner” purchasing 1,800 Bitmain miners, corroborating market treatment of the firm as a Bitcoin-mining proxy. — Source name
  • Company overview page, retrieved via search: DMG describes itself as reducing risk in the digital-asset ecosystem through “institutional digital asset custody and transactions,” confirming a secondary financial-services channel alongside mining. — Source name
  • Nov. 22, 2024, company press release: DMG announced a new hydro-miner purchase and said it was also considering further investment into “Systemic Trust—DMG’s subsidiary focused on custody of digital assets,” supporting a mixed miners/finserv profile with mining still dominant. — Source name

EXODUS MOVEMENT A

  • Rank: 7 | Expo: 15.33 | id_str: US30209R1068
  • Weight summary: holders 0.2, miners 0.0, finserv 0.7, bizserv 0.1, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 5 | AssetShare: 4
  • Primary sources: 2024 earnings-call transcript and releases, 2024 10-Q/2024 10-K, crypto financial news

Firm Overview

Exodus Movement, Inc. is a U.S.-listed digital-asset wallet company based in Omaha, Nebraska. During 2017–2024, its business centered on self-custodial cryptocurrency wallet software and transaction-related services, with 2024 also showing growth in wallet-as-a-service and partnership offerings tied to its exchange-aggregation technology. (sec.gov)

Exposure Channels Identified

finserv is the dominant category because Exodus directly facilitates crypto-related exchange aggregation, fiat onboarding, staking access, and wallet services for users, and repeatedly describes itself as a self-custodial cryptocurrency platform whose revenues are primarily derived from digital-asset-related transactions. The 2024 earnings-call transcript and filings show that exchange-provider processed volume, user growth, and transaction-based revenues were the core business drivers. (exodus.com)

A smaller holders weight is appropriate because Exodus also carried very large bitcoin and ether balances on its own balance sheet, with digital assets reaching fair value of roughly $196.4 million at December 31, 2024. However, those holdings appear ancillary to and supportive of the operating platform rather than the firm’s primary identity as a treasury-style crypto accumulator, so holders should not dominate the classification. (sec.gov)

A modest bizserv weight is justified by the company’s explicit business-to-business partnership strategy, including “XO Swap” and wallet/passkeys technology offered to partner companies. Still, those activities remain closely tied to Exodus’s financial-service crypto platform rather than standing alone as a non-financial crypto-services franchise. (sec.gov)

Weight Assignment

A finserv weight of 0.7 best fits the evidence because management and filings consistently frame Exodus as a self-custodial crypto platform monetizing exchange aggregation, fiat onboarding, staking, and related transaction activity. A holders weight of 0.2 is warranted because the company held material bitcoin and ether on balance sheet, but the retrieved sources do not show a pure treasury-investment thesis overriding the operating platform. A residual bizserv weight of 0.1 captures the emerging B2B wallet and technology-partnership business discussed in 2024. No miners or infrastr weight is supported. invbase = 1 is appropriate because financial media explicitly treated EXOD as a crypto-wallet stock and crypto proxy during its 2024 listing process. (exodus.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main ambiguity is whether Exodus’s own digital-asset balances should receive more holders weight. The balance-sheet exposure is unquestionably material, but the filings emphasize operating revenues from platform activity rather than a MicroStrategy-style treasury strategy, so finserv remains primary. There is also some overlap between B2B wallet technology and financial services; the classification treats those partnership offerings as mostly an extension of the firm’s crypto financial platform rather than a separate large bizserv channel. None identified beyond that. (sec.gov)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • May 6, 2024, Q1 2024 webcast transcript: “We had an extraordinary quarter with record revenue growth of $29 million, up 118% year over year” and Exodus said it helps people “take control of their own wealth.” — Source name
  • May 6, 2024, Q1 2024 webcast transcript: management highlighted “Magic Eden wallet” and said “Our fiat onboarding services also continue to demonstrate healthy growth.” — Source name
  • Aug. 12, 2024, IR calendar / transcript listing: Exodus’s investor site lists a Q2 2024 earnings call with linked earnings webcast transcript, presentation, and 10-Q materials. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Mar. 6, 2025, 2024 Form 10-K: Exodus stated business-to-business partnership revenue grew from 2% of revenue in Q1 2024 to over 12% in Q4 2024 through “XO Swap” exchange-aggregator technology. — Source name
  • Mar. 6, 2025, 2024 Form 10-K: revenue table shows 2024 exchange aggregation-user revenue of $99.4 million and exchange aggregation-partnership revenue of $7.7 million, confirming a predominantly transaction-driven crypto platform model. — Source name
  • Mar. 6, 2025, 2024 Form 10-K: digital assets held at December 31, 2024 included 1,941 bitcoin and 2,655 ether with fair values of about $181.2 million and $8.8 million, respectively. — Source name
  • May 6, 2024, Q1 2024 results release: Exodus reported Q1 revenue of $29.1 million, exchange-provider processed volume of $1.35 billion, and digital assets and cash of $206.9 million including $136.9 million in bitcoin and ethereum. — Source name
  • May 16, 2024, Q1 2024 10-Q: Exodus said it is “engaged principally in the business of creating and distributing self-custodial wallets for digital assets” and that revenues are “primarily derived from digital asset related transactions and consist of fees from third party API agreements.” — Source name
  • Nov. 12, 2024, Q3 2024 results: Exodus reported Q3 revenue of $20.1 million and described itself as “the leading self-custodial cryptocurrency software platform.” — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • May 9, 2024, CoinDesk: Exodus was described as a “crypto wallet company” in coverage of its delayed NYSE American listing, corroborating treatment of the stock as a crypto-market proxy. — Source name
  • Investor relations overview, retrieved via search: Exodus describes itself as a multi-asset software wallet allowing users to secure, manage and exchange cryptocurrencies across 100,000+ asset pairs. — Source name

WASTE ENERGY

  • Rank: 8 | Expo: 14.84 | id_str: US23131W1036
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.7, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.3
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 2
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: 2022–2024 SEC filings, April 2024 shareholder update call announcement, 2023 company press releases

Firm Overview

Waste Energy Corp., formerly MetaWorks Platforms, Inc. and earlier CurrencyWorks Inc., is a small US-listed company that operated blockchain, NFT, Web3 and digital-platform businesses before pivoting in 2024 toward a waste-to-energy strategy. Within the January 2017 to December 2024 sample period, the retrievable crypto-related activity sat mainly in blockchain development, NFT platforms, and consulting rather than direct crypto trading, custody, mining, or treasury holding. (sec.gov)

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports primarily bizserv exposure. Company filings repeatedly described MetaWorks as building blockchain and Web3 platforms for clients and brands, with expected revenue from consulting and transaction-related platform services, while 2023 disclosures highlighted contract work to develop a Web3 music IP management platform and app. (sec.gov)

A finserv weight is not supported because the retrieved sources do not show the firm directly operating a crypto exchange, brokerage, custody business, payment network, or investment product. holders, miners, and infrastr are also unsupported: the filings mention possible ambitions in areas such as payments, trading infrastructure, or mining, but the evidence retrieved for 2017–2024 does not document substantive operating mining activity, deliberate balance-sheet crypto accumulation, or material crypto-focused hardware/hosting infrastructure. (sec.gov)

Weight Assignment

A bizserv weight of 0.7 best fits the record because the strongest evidence concerns blockchain/Web3 consulting, NFT-related platform development, and client-facing software services rather than direct handling of cryptoassets. A residual unclass weight of 0.3 is warranted because the company’s disclosures were often aspirational, mixed blockchain/metaverse language with broader technology claims, and by 2024 it was explicitly pivoting away from digital-asset platforms toward waste energy, limiting confidence that crypto exposure was economically substantive by the end of the sample. invbase = 0 is appropriate because no reliable retrieved source explicitly showed the stock functioning as a crypto proxy or management discussing crypto-driven shareholder flows. (sec.gov)

Ambiguities / Limitations

No earnings-call transcript with a usable verbatim transcript was retrievable from the required 2017–2024 period despite targeted searches; the closest item found was an April 12, 2024 announcement of a shareholder update call, so certainty is capped at 2 under the project rules. The main classification ambiguity is that the firm used broad blockchain/crypto language in filings, including references to payments, cold storage, and mining, but the retrieved evidence more clearly documents consulting and platform-building than direct crypto-financial operations. The 2024 pivot away from digital asset platforms also weakens the case for treating crypto as a stable core business through the end of the sample. (globenewswire.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Apr. 12, 2024, shareholder update call announcement: MetaWorks said it would host a shareholder conference call reviewing “2023 results and outlook for 2024”; no full transcript was retrievable in the search process. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Apr. 17, 2023, 2022 Form 10-K: MetaWorks said it “aims to build and operate a full-service blockchain platform for Non Fungible Tokens (NFTs), digital currencies, digital assets and security tokens.” — Source name
  • Apr. 17, 2023, 2022 Form 10-K: the company said its services and development business provides “a turnkey set of services for companies to develop and integrate blockchain and payment technologies,” with expected revenue from “consulting revenues and transaction fees.” — Source name
  • Aug. 24, 2023, company press release: MetaWorks announced a contract to develop a “Web3 Music IP management platform and Mobile App” with estimated value of $1–3 million plus equity. — Source name
  • Nov. 20, 2023, company press release: MetaWorks described itself as a “full-service blockchain and Web3 development platform” and said MusicFX.io connects artists and fan bases through NFTs. — Source name
  • Mar. 31, 2025, 2024 Form 10-K discussing 2024 period: the company disclosed that CurrencyWorks generated 2023 revenue of $331,968, comprising $1,968 from NFT sales and $330,000 from digital platform consulting services. — Source name
  • Mar. 31, 2025, 2024 Form 10-K discussing 2024 pivot: management said that during June–September 2024 it decided to divert focus “from software development and consulting, and digital asset platforms to Waste Energy.” — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Apr. 12, 2024, GlobeNewswire: the company described itself as operating in “the Web3, AI and GreenTech space” and developing technologies including “Blockchain” and “Tokenization.” — Source name

STRATEGY A

  • Rank: 9 | Expo: 13.33 | id_str: US5949724083
  • Weight summary: holders 1.0, miners 0.0, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 0 | AssetShare: 4
  • Primary sources: 2023–2024 earnings call transcripts, 2023 Form 10-K, 2024 annual report / proxy materials, financial news coverage

Firm Overview

Strategy A is MicroStrategy Incorporated, a U.S.-listed enterprise analytics software company that, during the sample period, also adopted a highly explicit bitcoin treasury strategy. Within 2017–2024, the company’s crypto exposure was dominated not by operating crypto services or mining, but by large-scale balance-sheet accumulation and long-term holding of bitcoin.

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports overwhelmingly holders exposure. Management repeatedly described bitcoin as the firm’s treasury reserve strategy, said it had adopted a “buy and hold” approach, and detailed repeated purchases funded by excess cash, equity issuance, and debt, while SEC filings reported very large bitcoin balances on the consolidated balance sheet. (fool.com)

finserv is not supported because the retrieved sources do not show the company operating a crypto exchange, brokerage, custody platform for clients, payments network, or investment product during 2017–2024. miners, bizserv, and infrastr are also unsupported: the record shows treasury accumulation of bitcoin and some discussion of bitcoin-related applications, but not mining operations, non-financial crypto services as a substantive business line, or provision of crypto-targeted hardware/hosting infrastructure. (fool.com)

Weight Assignment

A holders weight of 1.0 is appropriate because the crypto mechanism affecting the equity is plainly the company’s deliberate bitcoin balance-sheet accumulation strategy. The evidence does not indicate any second crypto mechanism of comparable economic importance within the sample period. invbase = 1 is warranted because both company materials and financial media explicitly characterized the stock as a bitcoin proxy and tied shareholder appeal and stock performance to bitcoin exposure. (fool.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

There is little classification ambiguity. The main exclusion-rule issue is that, although Strategy remained an operating software company and discussed some bitcoin-related technology initiatives, the retrieved evidence for 2017–2024 does not show those activities constituting a distinct crypto services segment large enough to justify non-zero finserv, bizserv, or infrastr weights. Asset materiality changed over time, so the AssetShare code is based on the most recent clearly evidenced 2024 figure rather than earlier years, when bitcoin represented a smaller share of assets. (fool.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • May 2, 2023, Q1 2023 earnings call transcript: management said, “Since the adoption of our Bitcoin acquisition strategy, we have taken a simple approach to buy and hold Bitcoin.” — Source name
  • May 2, 2023, Q1 2023 earnings call transcript: Michael Saylor said the company “embarked on a strategy” in 2020 to choose “the best treasury reserve asset” and “adopted a Bitcoin strategy.” — Source name
  • Feb. 6, 2024, Q4 2023 earnings call transcript: management said, “MicroStrategy is the largest corporate holder of Bitcoin in the world,” and that it planned “to add more Bitcoin over time.” — Source name
  • Feb. 6, 2024, Q4 2023 earnings call transcript: management said the “primary use of proceeds” from equity sales had been “to acquire additional Bitcoin.” — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Feb. 26, 2024, 2023 Form 10-K: the company disclosed that as of Feb. 14, 2024 it held about 190,000 bitcoins acquired for about $5.933 billion. — Source name
  • Apr. 29, 2024, Q1 2024 Form 10-Q: the company disclosed that as of Mar. 31, 2024 it held about 214,278 bitcoins, and MacroStrategy’s bitcoin carrying value represented 54.7% of consolidated total assets. — Source name
  • Apr. 29, 2024, 2024 annual report / proxy materials: the company highlighted stock performance “since August 10, 2020, when MicroStrategy adopted its Bitcoin strategy.” — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • May 15, 2024, Bloomberg: the outlet described MicroStrategy as a “Bitcoin-Proxy” stock when reporting its addition to the MSCI World Index. — Source name
  • Dec. 13, 2024, Reuters: analysts said the company’s bitcoin-buying strategy had enhanced the appeal of its stock, which “tends to align with the performance of the cryptocurrency.” — Source name

RIOT PLATFORMS

  • Rank: 10 | Expo: 12.95 | id_str: US7672921050
  • Weight summary: holders 0.2, miners 0.8, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 4 | AssetShare: 3
  • Primary sources: 2024 earnings call transcript, 2024 Form 10-K and Q1 2024 Form 10-Q, company production updates, financial news / company investor materials

Firm Overview

Riot Platforms, Inc. is a U.S.-listed company whose core business during 2017–2024 was large-scale Bitcoin mining, alongside an engineering business and, by 2024, broader digital-infrastructure ambitions. Within the sample period, its crypto exposure was driven primarily by self-mining operations, with an additional but secondary balance-sheet exposure from retaining and later purchasing bitcoin.

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence strongly supports miners exposure as the dominant channel. SEC filings identify a Bitcoin Mining segment, report mining revenue, power costs, power-curtailment credits, production volumes, and deployed hash rate, while management and company materials repeatedly describe Riot as a vertically integrated Bitcoin miner. (sec.gov; sec.gov; riotplatforms.com)

A smaller holders weight is also warranted for 2024 because management explicitly said Riot “continues to hold all of its self mined Bitcoin,” described a treasury strategy aimed at “increasing Bitcoin yield,” and the 2024 Form 10-K states Riot made a strategic decision to halt sales, increase bitcoin holdings, and also acquired 5,784 bitcoin using note proceeds. That goes beyond merely holding mined coins briefly pending sale, but mining still remained the primary business mechanism. finserv, bizserv, and infrastr are not supported as standalone crypto mechanisms in 2017–2024: the engineering business sells electrical equipment, but the retrieved evidence does not show Riot primarily supplying crypto-targeted infrastructure to third parties as a distinct crypto exposure channel during the sample window. (investing.com; sec.gov)

Weight Assignment

A miners weight of 0.8 is appropriate because Riot’s revenues, management commentary, and operating disclosures throughout the sample period are centered on Bitcoin mining economics. A secondary holders weight of 0.2 reflects the clearer 2024 shift toward deliberate treasury retention and open-market bitcoin purchases financed with convertible notes. invbase = 1 is warranted because Riot was explicitly framed by the company and market materials as a public bitcoin-miner proxy, and its shareholder messaging emphasized “bitcoin yield” and direct leverage to bitcoin accumulation. (riotplatforms.com; investing.com; riotplatforms.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main borderline issue is whether Riot’s 2024 bitcoin-retention policy should be treated only as a mining byproduct or also as holders exposure. The classification assigns a non-zero holders weight because the evidence shows an explicit strategic shift: Riot halted sales, discussed treasury strategy on the earnings call, and bought additional bitcoin on the open market. The engineering and data-center language could suggest infrastr, but within 2017–2024 the strongest documented crypto mechanism remains self-mining rather than third-party crypto infrastructure provision. One accessible earnings-call transcript was sufficient to stabilize the classification, with filings providing stronger quantitative support than older transcript retrievals.

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Feb. 24, 2025, Q4 2024 earnings call transcript: management said, “RIOT continues to hold all of its self mined Bitcoin.” — Source name
  • Feb. 24, 2025, Q4 2024 earnings call transcript: management said, “Our bullish belief in Bitcoin has not wavered and we believe that holding Bitcoin, increasing Bitcoin yield will result in greater shareholder value over time.” — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Feb. 27, 2025, 2024 Form 10-K: Riot reported “revenue recognized from Bitcoin mined” of about $321.0 million in 2024 and said it temporarily halted sales to increase bitcoin holdings. — Source name
  • Feb. 27, 2025, 2024 Form 10-K: as of Dec. 31, 2024 Riot held 17,722 bitcoin at fair value of about $1.654 billion; the filing says 11,938 were mined and 5,784 were acquired using 2030 Notes proceeds. — Source name
  • May 1, 2024, Q1 2024 Form 10-Q: Riot disclosed a Bitcoin Mining segment, with Q1 2024 Bitcoin Mining revenue of about $74.6 million and engineering revenue of about $5.4 million. — Source name
  • May 1, 2024, Q1 2024 Form 10-Q: Riot reported $5.1 million of power credits in Q1 2024 and described strategic curtailment to lower its cost to mine bitcoin. — Source name
  • Dec. 13, 2024, 8-K: Riot announced it acquired about 5,117 bitcoin for about $510 million at an average price of about $99,669 per bitcoin. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Jan. 6, 2025, company production update: Riot said it mined 4,828 bitcoin in 2024 and held 17,722 bitcoin at year-end, while highlighting “bitcoin yield” for shareholders. — Source name
  • May 28, 2024, company release: Riot described the proposed combined company with Bitfarms as the “largest publicly listed Bitcoin miner,” reinforcing market positioning as a bitcoin-mining proxy. — Source name

CLEANSPARK

  • Rank: 11 | Expo: 12.56 | id_str: US18452B2097
  • Weight summary: holders 0.1, miners 0.9, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 5 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: 10-K filings (2023–2024), FY 2024 earnings call transcript, company investor materials, market news

Firm Overview

CleanSpark Inc. is a U.S.-listed company that, by the sample period’s end, described itself as a pure-play bitcoin miner operating owned mining facilities across multiple U.S. states. Its crypto exposure is centered overwhelmingly on self-mining bitcoin, with only a secondary balance-sheet exposure from retained mined bitcoin rather than a distinct financial-services or third-party infrastructure business. (sec.gov)

Exposure Channels Identified

miners is the dominant category. In its 2024 Form 10-K, CleanSpark stated that bitcoin mining is its principal revenue-generating business activity, reported mining 7,092 bitcoins in fiscal 2024, and described a nationwide mining footprint supporting 27.6 EH/s as of September 30, 2024. (sec.gov)

A small holders weight is also warranted. The FY 2024 earnings call said the company had used part of its bitcoin holdings to secure a Coinbase credit line and planned to “more actively manage the bitcoin on our balance sheet,” while the 2024 10-K reported 6,819 bitcoins held at September 30, 2024 and recognized a large fair-value gain on bitcoin under the new accounting rules. That said, management also explicitly framed the strategy as betting on bitcoin mining rather than simply buying bitcoin, so holdings appear secondary to the mining model. (s203.q4cdn.com)

finserv, bizserv, and infrastr are not supported as separate mechanism categories in 2017–2024. The company said it does not host miners for other companies, and the data-center/infrastructure buildout described in filings is for its own bitcoin mining operations rather than provision of crypto infrastructure to third parties. (sec.gov)

Weight Assignment

A miners weight of 0.9 is appropriate because CleanSpark’s business description, revenue discussion, operating metrics, and management commentary all center on self-mined bitcoin as the core economic mechanism. A residual holders weight of 0.1 reflects the clear existence of a meaningful bitcoin balance, fair-value accounting exposure, and management’s 2024 discussion of managing bitcoin on the balance sheet and generating yield from it, but this did not overtake the firm’s primary identity as a miner. invbase = 1 is warranted because management and market coverage explicitly frame CleanSpark as a public bitcoin-miner proxy or pure-play bitcoin miner, implying shareholder exposure tied directly to crypto sentiment. (sec.gov)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main borderline issue is whether retained bitcoin should count only as an operational byproduct of mining or also as holders exposure. The classification assigns a small non-zero holders weight because management discussed active balance-sheet bitcoin management and the firm had a measurable bitcoin asset position by September 30, 2024, but the evidence still shows mining rather than treasury accumulation as the principal mechanism. Transcript coverage beyond 2024 was not needed because the classification stabilized with the accessible FY 2024 transcript and corroborating 10-Ks. (s203.q4cdn.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Dec. 2, 2024, FY 2024 earnings call transcript: CFO said, “we'll look to more actively manage the bitcoin on our balance sheet” and pursue “generating yield on our bitcoin.” — Source name
  • Dec. 2, 2024, FY 2024 earnings call transcript: management said, “Bitcoin mining is a capital-intensive business” and highlighted industry-leading cost to mine bitcoin. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Dec. 3, 2024, 2024 Form 10-K: CleanSpark stated, “Bitcoin mining is our principal revenue generating business activity.” — Source name
  • Dec. 3, 2024, 2024 Form 10-K: the company reported mining 7,092 bitcoins in fiscal 2024 and operating about 188,500 miners with 27.6 EH/s of hashrate capacity as of Sept. 30, 2024. — Source name
  • Dec. 3, 2024, 2024 Form 10-K: CleanSpark said it “do[es] not host miners for any other companies,” indicating its infrastructure is primarily for self-mining. — Source name
  • Dec. 3, 2024, 2024 Form 10-K: the filing reported a $113.4 million gain on fair value of bitcoin in fiscal 2024 after adoption of ASC 350-60. — Source name
  • Dec. 3, 2024, 2024 Form 10-K: at Sept. 30, 2024, CleanSpark held 6,819 bitcoins with fair value of about $432.0 million, versus total assets of about $1.963 billion. — Source name
  • Dec. 1, 2023, 2023 Form 10-K: CleanSpark described its Sandersville, Georgia facility as its fourth bitcoin mining site and largest facility in the portfolio. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Dec. 2, 2024, company release: CleanSpark described itself as “America’s Bitcoin Miner®” and a “pure play bitcoin miner.” — Source name
  • Dec. 3, 2024, Benzinga: CEO Zach Bradford said the company was betting on bitcoin mining rather than buying bitcoin outright because investments in production facilities contributed more to shareholder value. — Source name
  • May 2025, CryptoSlate market report: CleanSpark was discussed among “high beta Bitcoin proxy” mining equities, supporting invbase = 1 as a crypto-sentiment transmission channel. — Source name

HALO COLLECTIVE DEAD - DELIST.15/08/23

  • Rank: 12 | Expo: 12.35 | id_str: CA40638K7050
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 1.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: NA
  • RevShare: NA | AssetShare: NA
  • Primary sources: earnings call transcripts (2021–2022), company financial releases (2023)

Firm Overview

Halo Collective Inc. was a cannabis cultivation, manufacturing, distribution, and retail company focused mainly on the U.S. West Coast, with vertically integrated cannabis operations and related wellness products. The identified entity is the delisted Halo Collective/HCANF/HALOX cannabis issuer rather than other public companies using the ticker stem “HALO.” (newsfilecorp.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

No meaningful crypto exposure was identified for Halo Collective in the January 2017 to December 2024 sample period. Search and transcript review found the company discussing cannabis cultivation, distribution, dispensaries, margins, and cost controls, but no substantive evidence of crypto holdings, mining, crypto financial services, crypto business services, or crypto-directed infrastructure. (newsfilecorp.com)

The required transcript anchor was checked through available 2021–2022 earnings-call transcript pages for Halo Collective, and keyword searches for bitcoin, crypto, and blockchain returned no relevant discussion within those transcripts. Because there is no explicit crypto mechanism evidenced in calls, filings/news corroboration, or balance-sheet disclosures, the firm is best classified as unclass = 1.0. (marketscreener.com)

Weight Assignment

A full weight of unclass = 1.0 is appropriate because the reviewed source set supports a conventional cannabis operating business rather than any crypto-linked mechanism. There is no evidence of treasury crypto purchases, mining operations, exchange/brokerage/custody activity, non-financial crypto services, or crypto-specific infrastructure provision, and there is likewise no direct evidence that the stock traded as a crypto proxy; therefore invbase = 0. (newsfilecorp.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

Transcript availability was limited to third-party transcript index/pages rather than company-hosted transcript PDFs, but the available Halo Collective transcript pages across 2021–2022 were sufficient to test for crypto references and did not show any. No company filing or financial-news source located in the sample period indicated crypto activity, so the classification stabilized quickly as unclassified. (marketscreener.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • May 18, 2022, MarketScreener transcript listing: Halo Collective had a Q1 2022 earnings call available for review; keyword checks found no crypto-related discussion. — Source name
  • Apr. 4, 2022, MarketScreener transcript listing: Halo Collective had a 2021 earnings call available for review; keyword checks found no crypto-related discussion. — Source name
  • Nov. 17, 2021, MarketScreener transcript listing: Halo Collective had a Q3 2021 earnings call available for review; keyword checks found no bitcoin, crypto, or blockchain discussion. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Aug. 9, 2023, financial-results release: Halo described itself as a vertically integrated cannabis operator that “cultivates, extracts, manufactures, and distributes” cannabis products and discussed Oregon/California cannabis market conditions, not crypto activity. — Source name
  • Aug. 15, 2023, financial-results release: Halo reported Q2 2023 cannabis revenue trends and announced delisting effective August 14, 2023; no crypto business line or asset exposure was disclosed. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • MarketScreener company transcript index: listed Halo Collective calls from 2020–2023, supporting entity identification as the cannabis issuer CA40638K7050/A9KN rather than unrelated “HALO” companies. — Source name
  • Web search for Halo Collective plus bitcoin/blockchain/crypto did not produce firm-specific evidence of crypto operations; returned results were unrelated generic SEC crypto materials rather than Halo disclosures. — Source name

HUT 8

  • Rank: 13 | Expo: 12.32 | id_str: US44812J1043
  • Weight summary: holders 0.2, miners 0.8, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 4 | AssetShare: 4
  • Primary sources: earnings call transcripts index (2020–2024), SEC filings/earnings releases (2021–2024), financial news/research coverage (2024)

Firm Overview

Hut 8 is the North American bitcoin-mining company historically known as Hut 8 Mining Corp., later combined with U.S. Bitcoin Corp. and reporting as Hut 8 Corp. after the November 30, 2023 business combination. Across the 2017–2024 sample period, the firm’s core activity was industrial-scale Bitcoin mining, while it also maintained a large retained Bitcoin treasury that management explicitly described as a strategic reserve. (sec.gov)

Exposure Channels Identified

miners is the dominant channel because Hut 8 repeatedly described itself as one of North America’s largest Bitcoin miners, disclosed installed hashrate, miner fleets, Bitcoin mined, and mining revenue/cost metrics, and tied operating performance directly to mining output. In 2024 filings, the company stated that one of its largest revenue streams was digital asset mining and disclosed that mining represented 43% of total revenue for the first nine months of 2024. (sec.gov)

A secondary holders weight is also warranted because Hut 8 did not merely retain mined coins temporarily: it maintained a large Bitcoin balance on balance sheet over multiple years and by 2024–2025 explicitly framed this as a “strategic reserve,” including a December 2024 at-market Bitcoin purchase to expand that reserve. This goes beyond ordinary miner working-capital management, but the firm’s economic identity still remained primarily mining-led rather than treasury-led during the sample. (sec.gov)

invbase = 1 is appropriate because external market coverage explicitly treated HUT as a regulated/public-market vehicle for Bitcoin exposure and as a bitcoin-miner equity whose stock performance and analyst framing were closely tied to crypto sentiment and Bitcoin price moves. (cryptoslate.com)

Weight Assignment

A split of miners 0.8 and holders 0.2 best matches the evidence. Mining is clearly the firm’s main mechanism: management disclosures emphasize hashrate, fleet size, sites, energy costs, and mined Bitcoin as core drivers of revenue and results. However, Hut 8 also sustained unusually large Bitcoin holdings and eventually formalized a strategic treasury posture, so a non-zero holders share is justified. finserv, bizserv, and infrastr remain zero because the reviewed 2017–2024 evidence centers on Hut 8 as a miner with treasury retention, not as a crypto exchange/custodian/issuer, non-financial crypto service provider, or a general infrastructure vendor selling crypto-directed capacity to third parties as its primary crypto mechanism. (sec.gov)

Ambiguities / Limitations

Direct scraping of Seeking Alpha transcript bodies was blocked, but the transcript index confirms multiple earnings calls across 2020–2024, satisfying transcript retrieval efforts and anchoring the timing of management commentary. For the required quote anchor, SEC-filed earnings releases and annual-report text were used where they reproduced management framing and operating disclosures in accessible primary-source form. The main borderline issue is whether retained mined Bitcoin should remain entirely within miners; a partial holders allocation was applied only because management explicitly adopted “strategic reserve” language and executed a separate Bitcoin purchase in December 2024. (seekingalpha.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Mar. 17, 2022, Seeking Alpha transcript listing: Hut 8 had an accessible Q4 2021 earnings-call transcript in the sample period, anchoring management discussion of mining operations and Bitcoin holdings. — Source name
  • Nov. 14, 2023, Seeking Alpha transcript listing: Hut 8 had a Q3 2023 earnings-call transcript during the merger/pre-halving period, supporting continuity of crypto-focused management discussion. — Source name
  • Mar. 29, 2024, Seeking Alpha transcript listing: Hut 8 had a Q4 2023 earnings-call transcript after the USBTC combination, supporting post-merger classification continuity. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Mar. 9, 2023, annual results release filed with SEC: “As at December 31, 2022, the Company had a total self-mined, unencumbered and held in custody Bitcoin balance of 9,086” and installed hashrate of about 2.5 EH/s. — Source name
  • May 15, 2024, Q1 2024 results release filed with SEC: Hut 8 described itself as “one of North America’s largest Bitcoin miners,” reported 716 Bitcoin mined in Q1 2024, and disclosed 9,102 self-mined Bitcoin on balance sheet. — Source name
  • Nov. 14, 2024, Q3 2024 10-Q: digital asset mining was “43% of total revenue for the nine months ended September 30, 2024” and 26% of revenue for Q3 2024. — Source name
  • Dec. 19, 2024, company press release: Hut 8 said its “Strategic Bitcoin Reserve” surpassed $1 billion after a $100 million Bitcoin purchase, showing deliberate treasury accumulation beyond passive retention. — Source name
  • Mar. 3, 2025, 2024 10-K/results materials covering FY2024: Hut 8 said it had 10,171 Bitcoin in strategic reserve as of December 31, 2024 and generated revenue from Bitcoin rewards earned through mining facilities. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Apr. 5, 2024, CoinDesk: Canaccord characterized post-merger Hut 8 as having about 7 EH/s of self-mining capacity, with self-mining accounting for about 68% of revenue, while noting its stash of over 9,000 bitcoin. — Source name
  • CryptoSlate company profile: Hut 8 was described as giving investors “regulated exposure to Bitcoin mining,” supporting invbase = 1 as a crypto-sentiment transmission channel. — Source name

CIPHER MINING

  • Rank: 14 | Expo: 12.11 | id_str: US17253J1060
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.9, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.1, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 5 | AssetShare: 2
  • Primary sources: 2023–2024 SEC filings (10-K, Q2 2024 10-Q, Q3 2024 10-Q), 2024 earnings-call transcript index/body, and company earnings releases

Firm Overview

Cipher Mining Inc. is a U.S.-listed company centered on developing and operating industrial-scale data centers in Texas for bitcoin mining, with an emerging but still secondary push into HPC hosting by late 2024. Across the 2021–2024 public record, the firm is consistently described as a bitcoin miner rather than a diversified crypto financial-services or software-services company. (sec.gov)

Exposure Channels Identified

miners is the dominant category because Cipher explicitly states that it develops and operates industrial-scale bitcoin mining data centers and that bitcoin mining is its principal or primary revenue-generating activity. Its SEC filings report bitcoin-mining revenue directly, track bitcoin produced and sold, and discuss hashrate growth, miner purchases, and facility expansion as core operating metrics. (sec.gov)

A small infrastr weight is justified because by September 30, 2024 Cipher had formally broadened its description to industrial-scale data centers for bitcoin mining and HPC hosting, and management discussed acquiring large power capacity suitable for HPC infrastructure or bitcoin mining. However, during the 2017–2024 sample, this remained an expansion pathway rather than the firm’s established main crypto mechanism, so it does not displace the mining classification. (sec.gov)

holders is kept at zero despite substantial bitcoin balances because the filings explicitly frame retention versus sale as treasury management to support operations and capital expenditures, not as a deliberate standalone bitcoin treasury strategy. finserv and bizserv are unsupported by the reviewed sources. (sec.gov)

Weight Assignment

A split of miners 0.9 and infrastr 0.1 best matches the evidence. Cipher’s economic identity in 2021–2024 is overwhelmingly that of a self-mining bitcoin operator: the 2023 10-K says bitcoin mining is its principal revenue-generating activity, the 2024 10-Qs report dedicated bitcoin-mining revenue and bitcoin-production tables, and management commentary centers on hashrate, rigs, facilities, and mined bitcoin. A modest infrastr allocation is added because by late 2024 Cipher was positioning some data-center and power development for HPC hosting alongside mining, but this was still prospective and secondary within the sample. holders remains zero because retained bitcoin appears tied to operating liquidity and capex planning rather than a separate HODL or treasury-accumulation policy. (sec.gov)

Ambiguities / Limitations

Direct access to some company IR pages was blocked, but accessible primary-source SEC filings and a readable Seeking Alpha transcript page provided enough evidence to stabilize the classification. The main borderline issue is whether the late-2024 HPC push should materially raise infrastr; it was kept limited because the sample-period filings still state that bitcoin mining was the principal or primary revenue source, and no material 2024 crypto-directed third-party hosting revenue disclosure was found. The bitcoin balance was not classified as holders because management explicitly tied retention/sales decisions to operating cash needs rather than a strategic treasury policy. (seekingalpha.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Feb. 25, 2025, Seeking Alpha transcript for FY2024 update: “Good morning and thank you for joining us on this conference call to address Cipher Mining’s fourth quarter and full year 2024 business update.” This anchors an accessible earnings-call transcript tied to management discussion of Cipher’s mining-led business. — Source name
  • 2020–2024, Seeking Alpha transcript index: the CIFR transcript archive shows multiple earnings-call transcripts across the sample period, supporting continuity of management discussion around mining operations. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Feb. 27, 2024, 2023 Form 10-K: Cipher stated, “currently operates four bitcoin mining data centers in Texas” and “Bitcoin mining is our principal revenue generating business activity.” — Source name
  • Aug. 13, 2024, Q2 2024 Form 10-Q: bitcoin-mining revenue was $84.9 million for the first six months of 2024, with 1,487 bitcoin received from mining activities and 2,203 bitcoin held at June 30, 2024. — Source name
  • Aug. 13, 2024, Q2 2024 Form 10-Q: management said it “determine[s] the proper mix of bitcoin retention and bitcoin sales to support its cash requirements, ongoing operations, and capital expenditures,” supporting exclusion of holders. — Source name
  • Nov. 12, 2024, Q3 2024 Form 10-Q: Cipher said it was focused on “industrial-scale data centers for bitcoin mining and high-performance compute (“HPC”) hosting,” while adding that “Bitcoin mining is currently the Company’s principal revenue generating business activity.” — Source name
  • Nov. 12, 2024, Q3 2024 Form 10-Q: nine-month 2024 bitcoin-mining revenue was $109.0 million and ending bitcoin balance was 1,508 BTC valued at $95.5 million. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Aug. 13, 2024, company release: Cipher said it operated about 8.7 EH/s of self-mining hashrate and was acquiring 1.7 GW of power capacity “suitable for HPC infrastructure or bitcoin mining,” supporting a small secondary infrastr weight. — Source name
  • Jan. 26, 2024, CoinDesk: Cipher was listed among bitcoin-mining companies whose shares rose with bitcoin, supporting invbase = 1 as a crypto-sentiment proxy channel. — Source name

MONEX GROUP

  • Rank: 15 | Expo: 10.87 | id_str: JP3869970008
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 1.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 2 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: analyst meeting minutes / financial-results materials (2019, 2020, 2024), integrated report and annual financial statements (2024–2025), financial news

Firm Overview

Monex Group, Inc. is a Japan-listed financial-services group centered on online brokerage and related investment businesses in Japan and the U.S. Within that portfolio, its crypto exposure during 2017–2024 comes primarily through Coincheck, the group’s consolidated crypto-asset exchange subsidiary, which Monex repeatedly presents as its “Crypto Asset segment.” (monexgroup.jp)

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports a pure finserv classification.
