Monex’s crypto exposure is not through treasury holdings or mining, but through operating a regulated crypto exchange and related custody/trading services via Coincheck; management materials explicitly describe Coincheck as a “leading crypto asset trading platform in Japan” and show a distinct “Crypto Asset segment” with trading income and other revenues. (monexgroup.jp)

The same materials also show that Monex framed Coincheck as a core profit contributor across multiple years, with earnings-call/analyst-meeting language tying group performance to “cryptoassets and Coincheck businesses,” and later presentations highlighting marketplace, exchange, OTC, custody, IEO, staking, and OnRamp services. Those are direct crypto financial services rather than non-financial support activities, so bizserv and infrastr remain zero. (monexgroup.jp)

Weight Assignment

A full finserv 1.0 is the best fit. Across the reviewed 2019, 2020, and 2024 earnings-call/analyst materials, Monex consistently treats Coincheck as the group’s crypto-asset operating business, with revenues linked to customer crypto trading and with services including exchange, marketplace, OTC, custody, IEO and staking. No reviewed source shows Monex as a crypto miner, a strategic balance-sheet crypto holder, or a hardware/compute infrastructure provider. Because the crypto mechanism is entirely mediated through exchange, custody, and transaction-based digital-asset services, the classification is wholly finserv. (monexgroup.jp)

Ambiguities / Limitations

There is no meaningful mechanism ambiguity: the main issue was source format rather than substance, because accessible “earnings call” evidence came through Monex’s analyst meeting minutes / presentation transcripts rather than a conventional Seeking Alpha transcript. That still satisfies the transcript anchor requirement because the documents record management’s spoken investor-facing discussion. invbase is set to 1 because financial media explicitly described Monex shares as tracking Bitcoin and reacting to Coincheck-related crypto profitability, but this is treated as an amplifier only and not as a mechanism category. (monexgroup.jp)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • May 7, 2020, Analyst Meeting Minutes for FY ended March 2020: “The major drivers of this 20% growth were cryptoassets and Coincheck businesses.” This is a direct management statement tying group earnings to Coincheck’s crypto operations. — Source name
  • Apr. 25, 2019, Financial Results presentation for FY ended March 2019: Monex discussed the “Crypto Asset Segment,” including Coincheck’s assets in custody, OTC trading, and blockchain-related initiatives. — Source name
  • Jul. 26, 2024, Financial Results for 1Q FY ending March 31, 2025: Monex reported a dedicated “Crypto Asset segment,” noting subdued Coincheck customer transactions but continued segment disclosure. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Integrated Report 2024: Monex states that verified accounts at Coincheck are a key indicator “that drives growth in our securities and crypto asset businesses,” confirming crypto as an operating business line. — Source name
  • Annual Financial Statements (Term March 2025): Monex says Coincheck’s “primary source of revenue is the spread income (trading profit) generated from customers’ crypto asset transactions,” and describes Coincheck’s services for retail, corporate and institutional clients. — Source name
  • Oct. 28, 2024, Data Book for 2Q FY ending March 31, 2025: Monex disclosed Coincheck verified accounts, exchange trading value, and other crypto-operating metrics as a separate business line. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Jul. 28, 2021, CoinDesk: Monex reported first-quarter profit driven largely by its crypto division and linked the performance to higher Coincheck trading volumes. — Source name
  • Feb. 24, 2021, Bloomberg: Monex shares were described as tracking Bitcoin because the firm owned Coincheck, supporting invbase = 1 as a crypto-sentiment proxy channel. — Source name
  • Apr. 6, 2018, Reuters via The Straits Times: Monex’s purchase of Coincheck triggered a sharp share-price reaction, consistent with investor-base sensitivity to crypto developments. — Source name

FRMO

  • Rank: 16 | Expo: 10.83 | id_str: US30262F2056
  • Weight summary: holders 0.4, miners 0.6, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: 2023–2024 quarterly conference-call transcripts, 2022–2024 annual reports, company crypto exposure summary

Firm Overview

FRMO Corp. is a U.S.-listed financial company based in White Plains, New York, with activities spanning investment holdings, advisory/consulting income, and a portfolio of securities and alternative assets. Within the 2017–2024 sample, crypto exposure appears through both direct/indirect digital-asset holdings and active cryptocurrency mining operations rather than through exchange, custody, or other client-facing crypto financial services. (frmocorp.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

Two mechanisms are clearly evidenced. First, FRMO is a holder because management explicitly discussed direct and indirect crypto holdings, stated that its “biggest exposure in crypto is clearly Bitcoin,” and the 2024 annual report recorded $10.98 million of digital assets on balance sheet as of May 31, 2024. (frmocorp.com)

Second, FRMO is also a miner because management said, “We want to be a cryptocurrency mining company,” described mining through Winland and Consensus Mining, and the annual reports state that the company generates “digital asset mining revenue” and owns digital mining assets used in mining operations. (frmocorp.com)

No evidence from the reviewed 2017–2024 sources supports finserv, bizserv, or infrastr as the primary mechanism categories under the project definitions. FRMO is not presented as a crypto exchange, broker, custodian, payments platform, or non-financial service provider to third-party crypto clients; its mining-related exposure is internal/investment exposure rather than selling infrastructure to the ecosystem. (frmocorp.com)

Weight Assignment

A split of miners 0.6 / holders 0.4 best matches the evidence. Management repeatedly frames the strategic intent as mining rather than becoming “a Bitcoin holding company,” which pushes the dominant mechanism toward miners; however, FRMO also has a meaningful and explicit balance-sheet holding component, including direct coin holdings and trust/ETF exposure summarized by the company and a $10.98 million digital-asset line item in the 2024 annual report. Because both channels are substantive and recurring, but mining is emphasized as the preferred business model, the final mix gives a modest majority weight to miners. (frmocorp.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main classification issue is overlap between mining and holding because FRMO both mines and owns crypto directly and indirectly. The exclusion rule for miners means temporarily retained mined coins alone would not justify holders, but FRMO’s transcript and holdings-summary evidence goes beyond that by showing explicit direct holdings, ETF/trust exposure, and management discussion of accumulating certain coins, so a mixed classification is warranted. No direct evidence was found that FRMO’s shareholder base trades the stock primarily as a crypto proxy, so invbase remains 0. (frmocorp.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Oct. 17, 2023, Q1 2024 conference call: “Our biggest exposure in crypto is clearly Bitcoin, mostly in the form of the Bitcoin Investment Trust...” — Source name
  • Apr. 16, 2024, Q3 2024 conference call: “We don’t want to be a Bitcoin holding company. We want to be a cryptocurrency mining company...” — Source name
  • Apr. 16, 2024, Q3 2024 conference call: management added that Winland and Consensus Mining “are mining Bitcoin, Litecoin, and Bitcoin Cash.” — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Aug. 15, 2024, annual report for FY ended May 31, 2024: FRMO reported “Digital assets, at fair value” of $10,978,796 and “Digital mining assets” of $1,139,972. — Source name
  • Aug. 15, 2024, annual report for FY ended May 31, 2024: “The Company primarily generates revenue through advisory, consulting fees and digital asset mining revenue.” — Source name
  • 2023 annual report for FY ended May 31, 2023: FRMO disclosed “cryptocurrency mining revenue” and listed investments in “Cryptocurrency Mining Entities,” including Consensus Mining and Seigniorage Corporation. — Source name
  • 2022 annual report for FY ended May 31, 2022: FRMO listed “Cryptocurrency Mining Entities” and described the merger of HK Cryptocurrency Mining vehicles into Consensus Mining & Seigniorage Corporation. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Feb. 29, 2024, FRMO crypto exposure summary: the company listed direct holdings including 155.69 Bitcoin, 1,968.67 Litecoin, 34.97 Ethereum, and indirect trust/ETF positions including GBTC and IBIT. — Source name
  • FRMO quarterly conference-call archive: company website shows recurring quarterly conference calls across 2017–2024, supporting use of management transcripts over the sample period. — Source name

MARA HOLDINGS

  • Rank: 17 | Expo: 10.30 | id_str: US5657881067
  • Weight summary: holders 0.3, miners 0.7, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 5 | AssetShare: 4
  • Primary sources: 2024 quarterly conference-call transcript, 2023–2024 SEC filings, company IR materials

Firm Overview

MARA Holdings, Inc. (formerly Marathon Digital Holdings) is a U.S.-listed digital asset technology company centered on large-scale Bitcoin mining and related compute operations. Within the 2017–2024 sample, its crypto exposure is overwhelmingly tied to mining, but by 2024 management also explicitly adopted a treasury strategy of retaining mined Bitcoin and making open-market Bitcoin purchases.

Exposure Channels Identified

The dominant channel is miners. MARA’s SEC filings describe the company as principally engaged in producing or “mining” digital assets with a focus on the Bitcoin ecosystem, and both filings and earnings-call remarks discuss Bitcoin production, mining rigs, hash rate growth, and mining economics in detail. (sec.gov; insidermonkey.com)

A secondary holders channel is also clearly supported. In the July 2024 earnings call, management said it had adopted a “full HODL approach,” planned strategic open-market Bitcoin purchases, and viewed Bitcoin as “the world’s best treasury reserve asset,” which goes beyond merely retaining mined coins temporarily for working capital. (insidermonkey.com)

No reviewed 2017–2024 evidence supports classification as finserv, bizserv, or infrastr under the project definitions. Although MARA develops and owns mining-related sites and compute capacity, the evidence indicates this infrastructure is primarily for its own mining activity rather than selling crypto infrastructure, custody, exchange, or non-financial support services to third-party crypto clients. (sec.gov)

Weight Assignment

A split of miners 0.7 / holders 0.3 best reflects the evidence. Mining is plainly the core operating business across the sample and accounts for essentially all clearly identified crypto-related revenue, which justifies the dominant miners weight. However, management’s explicit 2024 treasury-policy shift to retain all mined Bitcoin and periodically buy additional Bitcoin on the open market is substantive enough to warrant a meaningful holders allocation rather than treating all coin balances as merely incidental to mining operations.

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main overlap is between mined-coin retention and true treasury holding. Under the exclusion rule, operational retention of mined Bitcoin alone would not justify holders; here, however, the classification includes holders because management explicitly announced a “full HODL” policy, open-market Bitcoin purchases, and a treasury-reserve rationale in 2024. invbase is set to 1 because MARA is explicitly presented in financial commentary as a Bitcoin-proxy equity and management highlighted a verified retail shareholder question platform for earnings calls, though the strongest basis is the repeated market characterization of the stock as a crypto proxy rather than direct quantification of shareholder composition. (seekingalpha.com; ir.mara.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • July 30, 2024, Q2 2024 earnings call: “we announced a significant shift in our treasury policy and adopted a full HODL approach to retain all Bitcoin going forward.” — Source name
  • July 30, 2024, Q2 2024 earnings call: management added, “we plan to periodically make strategic open market purchases of Bitcoin” and called Bitcoin “the world’s best treasury reserve asset.” — Source name
  • July 30, 2024, Q2 2024 earnings call: “We produced an average of 22.9 Bitcoin each day during the quarter...” — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Feb. 28, 2024, Form 10-K for FY ended Dec. 31, 2023: MARA said it is “principally engaged in producing or ‘mining’ digital assets with a focus on the Bitcoin ecosystem.” — Source name
  • Feb. 28, 2024, Form 10-K for FY ended Dec. 31, 2023: the company mined 12,852 bitcoin in 2023 and operated about 210,000 mining rigs with 24.7 EH/s energized hash rate at year-end. — Source name
  • Feb. 26, 2025, 2024 results release / filing for FY ended Dec. 31, 2024: MARA disclosed that it had “adopted a treasury policy to retain all mined and purchased bitcoin for the foreseeable future.” — Source name
  • Feb. 26, 2025, Form 10-K for FY ended Dec. 31, 2024: cash was $391.8 million and fair value of digital asset holdings, including loaned and collateralized bitcoin, was $4.2 billion at Dec. 31, 2024. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • Mar. 2024 commentary: Seeking Alpha explicitly framed the stock as a “Bitcoin proxy,” evidencing crypto-investor-base spillover. — Source name
  • Apr. 24, 2024, investor-relations press release: MARA said verified retail and institutional shareholders could submit and upvote questions ahead of the earnings call. — Source name
  • Aug. 14, 2024, market coverage: Cointelegraph reported MARA bought 4,144 BTC and said the purchase brought its “strategic Bitcoin reserve” above 25,000 BTC. — Source name

VOYAGER DIGITAL (OTC)

  • Rank: 18 | Expo: 9.76 | id_str: CA92919V4055
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 1.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 5 | AssetShare: 4
  • Primary sources: earnings-call reporting on FY Q2 2022, CSE-filed MD&A (March 31, 2021), bankruptcy / regulatory coverage (2022–2023)

Firm Overview

Voyager Digital Ltd. was a publicly listed crypto brokerage and lending platform centered on retail customers, with operations built around digital-asset trading, custody-like account functionality, rewards, staking, and lending rather than mining or treasury accumulation. During the sample period, its crypto exposure sat squarely inside the operating business itself, culminating in its July 2022 Chapter 11 bankruptcy after losses tied to crypto lending counterparties.

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports a pure finserv classification. Management and contemporaneous reporting describe Voyager as a crypto platform / broker whose revenues came from transactions, lending, staking, and related customer crypto financial products rather than from selling software or infrastructure to third parties. In the February 15, 2022 earnings call, management said it was “investing heavily in revenue diversification” and CFO Evan Psaropoulos said roughly $87 million of quarterly revenue came from transactions and about $57 million from lending and staking, which directly indicates crypto financial-service fee income. The March 31, 2021 MD&A likewise shows fee revenue driven by trade volumes and interest revenue from custodians tied to customer assets and coins earning interest, further anchoring the business model as brokerage/lending/custody-style financial intermediation. No reviewed 2017–2024 evidence supports miners, bizserv, infrastr, or holders as separate mechanisms under the project rules.

Weight Assignment

A weight of finserv 1.0 best reflects the evidence. Voyager’s crypto exposure came from directly handling customer digital-asset trading, lending, staking, rewards, and related balances, and the available transcript-anchored evidence indicates these activities generated essentially all identifiable crypto-related revenue. Although Voyager also held large digital-asset balances, the evidence indicates these were operational balances linked to customer platform activity and lending rather than a discretionary treasury-investment or HODL policy, so holders remains 0.0.

Ambiguities / Limitations

Direct transcript pages were difficult to scrape from the primary transcript host, so the earnings-call anchor is taken from detailed contemporaneous reporting of the February 15, 2022 call rather than a fully fetched transcript page. That still provides a specific dated call quotation, but it is weaker than a directly scraped full transcript, so certainty is 3 rather than 4. invbase is set to 1 because major financial media repeatedly characterized Voyager as a crypto broker/lender and its equity plainly traded as a crypto-proxy name during the 2021–2022 cycle and collapse, though the strongest evidence is market characterization rather than management quantification of shareholder mix.

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Feb. 15, 2022, FY Q2 2022 earnings call: Steve Ehrlich said, “We are investing heavily in revenue diversification as we look to reduce our reliance on trading volume.” — Source name
  • Feb. 15, 2022, FY Q2 2022 earnings call: CFO Evan Psaropoulos said “Roughly $87 million in revenue came from transactions” and “About $57 million stemmed from lending and staking.” — Source name
  • Feb. 15, 2022, FY Q2 2022 earnings call: Blockworks reported Voyager had 3.2 million verified users, nearly 1.1 million funded accounts, and $5.9 billion of platform assets at Dec. 31, 2021. — Source name

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • May 25, 2021, MD&A for quarter ended Mar. 31, 2021: Voyager reported total revenue of $60.4 million for the quarter, with $53.7 million of fee revenue and $6.7 million of interest revenue. — Source name
  • May 25, 2021, MD&A for quarter ended Mar. 31, 2021: fee revenue rose primarily due to a $5.0 billion increase in trade volumes and higher average spread. — Source name
  • May 25, 2021, MD&A for quarter ended Mar. 31, 2021: interest revenue from custodians increased with AUM growth and “an increase in the overall number of coins earning interest.” — Source name
  • May 25, 2021, MD&A for quarter ended Mar. 31, 2021: the company held a net digital currencies and fiat position of $55.6 million and described digital currencies and fiat on loan as a credit-risk exposure. — Source name

Financial news / other:

  • July 6, 2022, Bloomberg: Voyager was described as a “crypto broker” that filed for Chapter 11 after market volatility and the collapse of a hedge fund borrower. — Source name
  • Oct. 12, 2023, Axios on CFTC action: the regulator described Voyager as a “crypto lender” and alleged it took excessive risks with customer assets to fund its rewards program. — Source name
  • July 1, 2022, CNBC: Voyager suspended trading, deposits, and withdrawals, with coverage describing it as a major crypto broker affected by broader crypto-market stress. — Source name

CANAAN ADR 1:15

  • Rank: 19 | Expo: 9.75 | id_str: US1347481020
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.4, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.6, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 5 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: 2020 and 2024 earnings-call materials, 2024 Form 20-F, market reporting on Canaan as a bitcoin-linked mining-rig stock

Firm Overview

Canaan Inc. is a Nasdaq-listed Singapore/Cayman-based technology company best known for designing and selling ASIC bitcoin-mining machines under the Avalon brand, with operations spanning chip design, miner sales, and an expanding self-mining business. During the January 2017 to December 2024 sample period, its crypto exposure came primarily from supplying mining hardware infrastructure to the bitcoin ecosystem, with a secondary direct exposure through operating its own bitcoin-mining fleet. (sec.gov)

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports a mix of infrastr and miners. Canaan repeatedly describes itself as deriving the great majority of revenue from sales of bitcoin mining machines, which fits the project’s infrastructure channel because it supplies purpose-built ASIC hardware used for mining. (sec.gov)

A separate miners weight is also warranted because management explicitly said in the November 30, 2020 earnings call that the company “operate[s] our own Bitcoin mining business,” and later filings quantify distinct mining revenue of US$32.5 million in 2022, US$34.0 million in 2023, and US$44.0 million in 2024. The evidence does not support finserv or bizserv, and the company’s bitcoin holdings appear tied mainly to self-mining operations rather than a separate treasury-allocation or HODL policy, so holders remains 0.0 under the exclusion rules. (fool.com)

Weight Assignment

A weight of infrastr 0.6 / miners 0.4 best reflects the economics disclosed across calls and filings. Infrastructure receives the larger share because miner sales were still the dominant business line throughout the sample and accounted for 82.6% of 2024 revenue, while self-mining was clearly material but secondary at 16.3% of 2024 revenue. The company is therefore not a pure miner, but neither is self-mining incidental enough to ignore. (sec.gov)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main classification question is whether bitcoin accumulated from self-mining should create a separate holders weight. The reviewed sources point instead to operational accumulation through mining: the 2025 results release says bitcoin accumulation was driven by mining, and the 2024 Form 20-F emphasizes mining as a “second engine” rather than a treasury strategy, so holders is excluded under the project rule for mined BTC held pending sale or operational use. invbase is set to 1 because market coverage explicitly treated Canaan shares as a bitcoin-linked mining-rig stock and management/filings acknowledge that the share price can rise or fall with bitcoin prices. (sec.gov)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Nov. 30, 2020, Q3 2020 earnings call: management said, “now we have the -- we operate our own Bitcoin mining business,” anchoring direct mining exposure. — The Motley Fool
  • Nov. 30, 2020, Q3 2020 earnings call: CEO Nangeng Zhang said, “when our sales are performing well, we only expect 10% to 20% of our mining machines to go our mining business,” showing self-mining alongside external machine sales. — The Motley Fool
  • Mar. 26, 2025, reporting on Q4/FY 2024 results: CFO James Cheng said, “Our mining business, which serves as our second growth engine, generated US$15.3 million in revenue,” and noted bitcoin accumulation through mining. — SEC Exhibit 99.1

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Dec. 31, 2024 Form 20-F: Canaan said it is “principally engaged in integrated circuit design and sale and lease of final mining equipment” for bitcoin mining. — SEC Form 20-F
  • Dec. 31, 2024 Form 20-F: sales of bitcoin mining machines and related parts were US$222.3 million in 2024, or 82.6% of total revenue. — SEC Form 20-F
  • Dec. 31, 2024 Form 20-F: mining revenue was US$32.5 million in 2022, US$34.0 million in 2023, and US$44.0 million in 2024. — SEC Form 20-F
  • Mar. 26, 2025 results release for FY 2024: owned cryptocurrency reached 1,292.5 bitcoins by year-end 2024. — SEC Exhibit 99.1

Financial news / other:

  • Feb. 16, 2021, Nasdaq: Canaan was described as a “cryptocurrency mining machine maker” whose stock jumped as bitcoin hit a record high, supporting crypto-proxy investor-base exposure. — Nasdaq
  • May 2022 annual-report language surfaced via proxy materials: the company warned that, because of its focus on bitcoin mining, its stock price may move with bitcoin prices. — Proxy materials / annual report extract

WEM ADE

  • Rank: 20 | Expo: 9.50 | id_str: KR7112040001
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 0.7, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.3
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: NA
  • Primary sources: 2021–2024 earnings-call materials / IR decks, 2023–2024 company disclosures, market reporting on WEMIX-linked share-price moves

Firm Overview

Wemade Co., Ltd. is a South Korea-listed game developer and publisher best known for the Legend of Mir franchise, but during the January 2017 to December 2024 sample period it also built a blockchain gaming ecosystem around the WEMIX token, WEMIX PLAY platform, wallets, DEX-related activity, and Web3 game services. The crypto exposure sits alongside its much larger core game-publishing business rather than replacing it, with reported blockchain revenue remaining a small but explicit line item in 2023–2024 company earnings materials. (file.wemade.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports a primary finserv classification. Wemade explicitly reports “blockchain” revenue, references WEMIX price assumptions for revenue recognition, states that blockchain revenue is recognized from unearned revenue, and tracks WEMIX PLAY transaction value, wallet users, and DEX activity in investor materials; that fits the project’s financial-services channel because the firm is directly operating tokenized platforms and services around its own digital asset ecosystem rather than merely providing outside support. (file.wemade.com)

A small unclass residual remains appropriate because the reviewed materials do not fully separate how much of the blockchain business is true crypto-financial-service economics versus broader game-platform or Web3 ecosystem monetization. The evidence does not support miners, holders, or infrastr: no mining operation was found, no separate treasury-style HODL policy was evidenced, and the company is not mainly supplying hardware or hosting to third-party crypto firms. (file.wemade.com)

Weight Assignment

A weight of finserv 0.7 / unclass 0.3 best matches the record. Finserv receives the majority because management repeatedly discloses blockchain-platform revenue, WEMIX-linked revenue recognition, wallet and DEX metrics, and a platform strategy centered on WEMIX PLAY and token-based services. Unclass retains 0.3 because blockchain revenue was still only a low-single-digit share of consolidated sales in the clearest 2024 disclosures, and the public materials do not always cleanly distinguish regulated-style crypto financial activity from broader blockchain-enabled game monetization. (file.wemade.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main boundary issue is whether Wemade should be treated as pure finserv or partly bizserv. The reviewed evidence points to finserv because the company directly runs token-linked wallets, platform transactions, and WEMIX-based ecosystem services, but some activity is embedded within game publishing and platform operations rather than disclosed as standalone custody/exchange revenue. No retrievable full verbatim third-party transcript page was available beyond company IR decks/presentations, so the earnings-call anchor relies on investor-presentation language rather than a classic call transcript webpage; for that reason certainty is 3 rather than 4. invbase is set to 1 because financial media explicitly linked movements in Wemade’s stock to WEMIX delisting news and crypto-related developments. (file.wemade.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Feb. 15, 2022, 4Q FY2021 earnings results: “WEMIX Wallet MAU(+832% QoQ) averaged 1.8 million in 4Q’21 and WEMIX DEX MAU(+872% QoQ) averaged 103 thousands,” anchoring direct wallet/DEX activity. — Wemade IR 4Q FY2021 Earnings Results
  • Feb. 15, 2022, 4Q FY2021 earnings results: “Quarterly WEMIX DEX transaction recorded 270 million USD in 4Q’21,” showing transaction-fee / platform activity around digital assets. — Wemade IR 4Q FY2021 Earnings Results
  • Feb. 7, 2024, 4Q FY2023 earnings results: “In 4Q’23, blockchain platform revenue spiked +276% QoQ as a result of elevated transaction value,” confirming an explicit blockchain revenue line. — Wemade IR 4Q FY2023 & Annual Earnings Results

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • May 8, 2024, 1Q FY2024 earnings release: “1Q’24 business-segment revenue” showed blockchain revenue of KRW 5,931 million versus total revenue of KRW 161,299 million, implying roughly 3.7% of sales. — Wemade IR 1Q FY2024 Earnings Results
  • May 8, 2024, 1Q FY2024 earnings release: blockchain revenue included WEMIX-related revenue recognized from unearned revenue, with a WEMIX reference price of about KRW 2,251. — Wemade IR 1Q FY2024 Earnings Results
  • Feb. 12, 2025, 4Q FY2024 & annual earnings results covering FY2024: the company said 4Q’24 blockchain revenue was KRW 997 million and that “Blockchain revenue is recognized from unearned revenue,” with a WEMIX base price of KRW 2,155. — Wemade IR 4Q FY2024 & Annual Earnings Results

Financial news / other:

  • Dec. 6, 2023, Korea JoongAng Daily: Wemade said it would build a WEMIX PLAY Center in Dubai and seek recognition of WEMIX as a crypto token under DIFC/DFSA frameworks, corroborating direct token/platform involvement. — Korea JoongAng Daily
  • Nov. 25, 2022, Korea JoongAng Daily: WEMIX delisting news coincided with Wemade stock falling about 30%, supporting investor-base spillover from crypto-specific developments. — Korea JoongAng Daily
  • Dec. 9, 2022, Maeil Business Pulse: after court approval of delisting, reporting tied Wemix-token events to movements in Wemade shares, further supporting invbase = 1. — Pulse

LM FUNDING AMERICA

  • Rank: 21 | Expo: 9.20 | id_str: US5020745031
  • Weight summary: holders 0.1, miners 0.9, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 4 | AssetShare: 2
  • Primary sources: 10-K filings (2022–2024), Q3 2024 and Q4 2024 earnings-call transcripts, company earnings releases

Firm Overview

LM Funding America, Inc. is a U.S.-listed company that historically operated a specialty-finance business but, by 2022–2024, had repositioned itself around Bitcoin mining through its US Digital Mining subsidiary. Company filings and earnings materials show that crypto exposure sits primarily in self-operated mining, with some balance-sheet Bitcoin retained from mining activity. (sec.gov)

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence clearly supports miners as the dominant channel. In its 2022 Form 10-K, LM Funding said it had “recently commenced cryptocurrency mining business” alongside its legacy finance activity and stated that it “commenced Bitcoin mining operations in late September 2022.” (sec.gov)

That mining exposure remains central in both filings and calls. The 2024 Form 10-K discusses Bitcoin mining revenue recognition, halving effects, and the economics of mining power costs, while the Q3 2024 earnings call described efforts to strengthen the firm’s “position in the Bitcoin mining landscape.” (sec.gov)

A small holders weight is also warranted because by 2024 the company explicitly discussed the value of its Bitcoin holdings and reported digital assets of roughly $14.0 million at December 31, 2024, with coins held both at a third-party custodian and in self-custody wallets. However, the filings also state that LM Funding sells Bitcoins “to pay for operating expenses and growth on an as-needed basis,” so the holdings appear mainly incidental to mining rather than a separate treasury-investment strategy. (investing.com)

Weight Assignment

A weight of miners 0.9 / holders 0.1 best fits the record. Mining receives the overwhelming majority because management repeatedly frames the company as a Bitcoin miner, reports Bitcoin mined and mining revenue, discusses hosting agreements, machine deployments, energy costs, and halving impacts, and in 2023 generated about $12.3 million of revenue from mining out of roughly $13.0 million total revenue. Holders receives only a residual 0.1 because the company did hold material Bitcoin on balance sheet by late 2024, but the evidence indicates those holdings were largely mined coin retained operationally rather than a distinct HODL-style treasury policy. (lmfunding.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main boundary issue is whether the balance-sheet Bitcoin should push the firm further into holders. The classification keeps holders small because the project rules exclude mined BTC held temporarily pending sale from being treated as true holders exposure unless there is evidence of a deliberate treasury-accumulation policy, which was not found in 2017–2024 materials. invbase is set to 1 because management explicitly compared LM Funding’s market capitalization to the value of its Bitcoin holdings on the Q4 2024 call, indicating that the stock was being framed to investors as a crypto proxy. (sec.gov)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Nov. 13, 2024, Q3 2024 earnings call transcript: “we continue to strengthen our position in the Bitcoin mining landscape,” directly anchoring mining as the core crypto activity. — Investing.com Q3 2024 transcript
  • March 31, 2025, Q4 2024 earnings call transcript covering FY2024: management said the firm’s market cap was below the value of its Bitcoin holdings, showing explicit investor framing around crypto exposure. — Investing.com Q4 2024 transcript

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Mar. 31, 2023, Form 10-K for FY2022: LM Funding said it “currently has two lines of business: our recently commenced cryptocurrency mining business and our historical specialty finance business.” — SEC FY2022 Form 10-K
  • Mar. 31, 2023, Form 10-K for FY2022: the company said it “commenced Bitcoin mining operations in late September 2022.” — SEC FY2022 Form 10-K
  • Mar. 31, 2025, Form 10-K for FY2024: the filing states the number of Bitcoin received was cut roughly 50% by the April 19, 2024 halving and separately discloses “Revenue Recognition - Bitcoin Mining.” — SEC FY2024 Form 10-K
  • Mar. 31, 2025, Form 10-K for FY2024: digital assets were approximately $14.0 million at year-end 2024, with about $11.6 million at a third-party custodian and $2.4 million in self-custody wallets. — SEC FY2024 Form 10-K
  • Mar. 31, 2025, Form 10-K for FY2024: the company states, “We sell our Bitcoins to pay for operating expenses and growth on an as-needed basis,” limiting the case for a larger holders weight. — SEC FY2024 Form 10-K
  • Mar. 28, 2024, FY2023 earnings release: LM Funding said it mined 423.4 Bitcoin in 2023, generating about $12.3 million in revenue based on an average Bitcoin price of $29,000. — LM Funding FY2023 earnings release
  • May 16, 2024, Q1 2024 earnings release: the company said reported Bitcoin holdings reached 155.1 Bitcoin through April 2024, valued at approximately $9.7 million. — LM Funding Q1 2024 earnings release

Financial news / other:

  • Aug. 13, 2024, Form 10-Q / company filing: LM Funding disclosed multiple hosting agreements with Core, GIGA, and Tech Infrastructure, confirming outsourced site/power arrangements in support of its mining fleet rather than financial-service activity. — LM Funding Q2 2024 Form 10-Q

ARGO BLOCKCHAIN

  • Rank: 22 | Expo: 8.61 | id_str: GB00BZ15CS02
  • Weight summary: holders 0.1, miners 0.9, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 5 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: 2021–2024 earnings call / earnings materials, 2023–2024 Form 20-F annual reports, company operating updates and annual results

Firm Overview

Argo Blockchain plc is a London-headquartered, Nasdaq- and LSE-listed cryptocurrency mining company whose operating business during 2017–2024 centered on mining Bitcoin through owned and hosted mining equipment in North America. Across its filings and earnings materials, crypto exposure appears overwhelmingly through self-mining economics rather than through financial services, software services, or broader infrastructure provision. (argoblockchain.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

The clear primary channel is miners. Management and filings repeatedly describe Argo as a cryptocurrency or Bitcoin mining company, report Bitcoin mined, mining margins, mining capacity, power and hosting costs, and the economics of operating mining fleets. (fool.com)

A small holders weight is also appropriate because Argo at times retained meaningful Bitcoin on balance sheet and explicitly referred to Bitcoin in treasury or “HODL.” However, the evidence indicates those holdings were largely mined coins managed as part of operating liquidity and collateral strategy rather than a distinct treasury-allocation business model; the 2023 and 2024 filings say mined Bitcoin was sold to fund operations or working capital, and by December 31, 2024 crypto represented less than 2% of total assets. (argoblockchain.com)

Weight Assignment

A weight of miners 0.9 / holders 0.1 best fits the evidence. Mining receives nearly all weight because Argo’s revenue was primarily derived from Bitcoin block rewards and transaction fees, and the company disclosed 2,045 Bitcoin mined in 2021, 2,156 in 2022, and 1,760 in 2023, alongside direct discussion of mining costs, hosting, and hashrate capacity. Holders receives only a residual 0.1 because Argo did at times maintain substantial Bitcoin balances and even described a treasury/HODL position, but the record across later filings shows those balances were secondary to the mining business and mainly tied to mined-coin working capital management rather than a separate HODL-style investment policy. (sec.gov)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main boundary issue is whether Argo’s historical Bitcoin treasury should justify a larger holders weight. That weight is kept small because the project rules exclude mined BTC held pending sale from counting as true holders exposure unless there is evidence of a deliberate treasury strategy materially separate from operations; Argo’s own materials emphasize selling mined Bitcoin for operating expenses, working capital, and collateral management. invbase is set to 1 because management explicitly discussed whether to sell Bitcoin versus finance growth on the 2021 earnings call, and financial media and market coverage during the period consistently framed Argo as a listed Bitcoin-miner proxy, implying investor demand was directly tied to crypto sentiment. (fool.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • May 2, 2022, Q4 2021 earnings call transcript: management said, “in 2021, we didn't sell much bitcoin... 2021, we didn't sell any,” while adding that going forward Argo would sell part of monthly production to cover operating costs and growth, anchoring both mining and only limited holders exposure. — The Motley Fool Q4 2021 transcript
  • May 2, 2022, Q4 2021 earnings call transcript: management discussed locating mining facilities in Quebec and the Texas panhandle and framed ESG around those mining operations, reinforcing that crypto exposure came through operating mines. — The Motley Fool Q4 2021 transcript
  • June 6, 2023, Q1 2023 earnings materials: Argo reported 85 Bitcoin or Bitcoin Equivalent in its “HODL,” but in the same results presentation emphasized quarterly BTC mined and mining margin percentage. — Argo Q1 2023 results

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Apr. 25, 2024, 2023 Annual Report / Form 20-F materials: “Cryptocurrency mining revenues are recognised at a point in time,” directly identifying mining as the revenue model. — Argo 2023 annual results PDF
  • Apr. 25, 2024, 2023 Annual Report / Form 20-F materials: Argo said it held 2,441 Bitcoin at the beginning of 2022 and used up to 1,504 Bitcoin as collateral with Galaxy Digital, showing treasury balances existed but were closely tied to financing and operations. — Argo 2023 annual results PDF
  • Apr. 30, 2024 / amended 2025, Form 20-F for FY2023: “In 2023, our treasury management strategy was to sell our mined Bitcoin on a weekly basis in order to fund our operating expenses and for working capital needs.” — SEC Form 20-F FY2023/A
  • Apr. 30, 2024 / amended 2025, Form 20-F for FY2023/A: Argo disclosed 2.8 exahash of mining capacity as of December 31, 2023, with approximately 2.4 exahash in hosted facilities and 0.4 exahash in owned Quebec facilities. — SEC Form 20-F FY2023/A
  • Apr. 30, 2024 / amended 2025, Form 20-F for FY2023/A: the company reported 2,045 Bitcoin mined in 2021, 2,156 in 2022, and 1,760 in 2023, with total revenues of $98.7 million, $58.5 million, and $50.6 million respectively. — SEC Form 20-F FY2023/A
  • May 2025, Form 20-F for FY2024: “Substantially all of our current business is focused on mining Bitcoin,” and revenue is “primarily comprised of the value of Bitcoin rewards and transaction fees we earn by mining the blockchain.” — SEC Form 20-F FY2024
  • May 2025, Form 20-F for FY2024: as of December 31, 2024, “less than 2% of our total assets were represented by our holdings of Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies in treasury,” limiting AssetShare and the holders weight. — SEC Form 20-F FY2024
  • Mar. 30, 2023, FY2022 annual results: Argo mined 2,156 BTC in 2022, reported revenue of $58.6 million, and disclosed total BTC held at December 31, 2022 of 141. — Argo 2022 full year results

Financial news / other:

  • Feb. 2022, company operational update: Argo reported 135 Bitcoin mined in February 2022 and stated its overall Bitcoin HODL was 2,685 Bitcoin, illustrating that balance-sheet Bitcoin existed but arose alongside the mining business. — Argo February 2022 operational update
  • Feb. 2023, financial news on investor litigation: coverage described Argo as a “Bitcoin mining” firm and tied investor claims to mining economics, capital constraints, and electricity costs, supporting invbase = 1 through market treatment as a crypto proxy. — Investing.com news item

COINSHARES INTERNATIONAL

  • Rank: 23 | Expo: 8.03 | id_str: JE00BLD8Y945
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 1.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 4 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: CoinShares earnings call transcripts (2021 Q4, 2022 Q4, 2023 Q1), annual reports (2020–2024), and company financial releases

Firm Overview

CoinShares International Limited is a Jersey-based listed digital-asset investment company and asset manager whose business during 2017–2024 centered on crypto ETP issuance, digital-asset asset management, trading/liquidity provision, and related capital-markets activity. Across company materials, crypto exposure sits directly inside regulated investment products and trading operations rather than mining, hardware, or non-financial support services. (s3.amazonaws.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports a pure finserv classification. Management repeatedly described CoinShares as a digital-asset asset manager, highlighted fee-generating AUM in its ETP platforms, and discussed custody fees, issuer expenses, and liquidity provision tied to its crypto investment products on earnings calls. (s3.amazonaws.com)

Annual reports corroborate that the majority of group revenue came from asset-management fees on ETPs, with additional gains and income from capital-markets activity conducted around digital assets and ETP support. While the group did hold some digital assets, the filings explicitly state that most digital-asset balances were held to hedge obligations to certificate holders, so those balances belong under finserv rather than holders under the project rules. (a.storyblok.com)

invbase is set to 1 because management explicitly discussed the company’s share-price trend on the Q1 2023 earnings call, and the business was consistently marketed and covered as a listed digital-asset manager and crypto ETP platform, making the stock a direct public-market crypto exposure vehicle. That is an inference from the company’s own investor communications plus market positioning, rather than from a single explicit “crypto proxy” quote. (s3.amazonaws.com)

Weight Assignment

A full 1.0 is assigned to finserv because the record across transcripts and annual reports is overwhelmingly concentrated in crypto financial services: ETP issuance, digital-asset asset management, custody-linked fee income, liquidity provision, and capital-markets activity. No evidence supports mining, infrastructure provision, or non-financial crypto services, and the company’s crypto asset holdings are mainly operational or hedging balances backing financial products rather than a separate treasury-investment strategy, so no residual holders weight is assigned. (s3.amazonaws.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The only boundary issue is whether CoinShares’ own digital-asset balances justify some holders exposure. That weight is kept at zero because the annual report states the group invests in digital assets and derivatives to match obligations to certificate holders and notes only some strategic balances above hedge requirements; on the project’s rules, crypto held to back or collateralise financial products belongs in finserv, and the filings do not show a dominant separate treasury/HODL policy. AssetShare is therefore kept low rather than high despite meaningful on-balance-sheet digital-asset activity. (a.storyblok.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Mar. 1, 2023, Q4 2022 earnings call transcript: management said CoinShares had “proved our leadership in the European Digital Asset Management business” and highlighted “properly structured and innovative ETPs,” directly anchoring crypto financial-services exposure. — CoinShares Q4 2022 transcript
  • Mar. 1, 2023, Q4 2022 earnings call transcript: the company said AUM across “the two ETP platforms, XBT Provider and CoinShares Physical stood at approximately £1 billion,” tying exposure to issued crypto investment products. — CoinShares Q4 2022 transcript
  • May 9, 2023, Q1 2023 earnings call transcript: CFO Richard Nash said “approximately 60%” of quarterly revenue came from asset management and “40%” from capital markets business. — CoinShares Q1 2023 transcript
  • May 9, 2023, Q1 2023 earnings call transcript: management described “management fees arising from the asset management platform in Q1 of 9.2 million pounds” and referred to custody fees and issuer expenses of the group’s ETPs. — CoinShares Q1 2023 transcript
  • May 9, 2023, Q1 2023 earnings call transcript: CEO Jean‑Marie Mognetti said CoinShares had refined its positioning “to become the premier alternative asset manager specialising in digital assets.” — CoinShares Q1 2023 transcript

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Apr. 2021, 2020 Annual Report: CoinShares disclosed that 79% of management fees were generated from ETPs referencing Bitcoin and 20% from Ethereum, showing crypto-product fee income was the core business. — CoinShares 2020 Annual Report
  • Apr. 2022, 2021 Annual Report: the group reported management fees of £80.4 million and also disclosed rewards from token staking and DeFi activities, confirming direct digital-asset financial operations. — CoinShares 2021 Annual Report
  • Apr. 2023, 2022 Annual Report: segment disclosure showed Asset management revenue of £50.1 million, Capital markets gains/income of £26.3 million, and Principal investment loss of £4.9 million. — CoinShares 2022 Annual Report
  • Apr. 2023, 2022 Annual Report: the report states “The majority of the Group’s revenue is from management fees on ETPs issued by XBTP and CSDSL.” — CoinShares 2022 Annual Report
  • Apr. 2023, 2022 Annual Report: CoinShares stated that it invests in digital assets and derivatives “to generate a return, which matches its financial obligations to certificate holders,” and that most digital-asset exposure is matched against those obligations. — CoinShares 2022 Annual Report
  • Apr. 1, 2025, 2024 Annual Report press release: CoinShares reported 2024 asset-management fees of £87.2 million, capital-markets gains and other income of £57.0 million, and described itself as a digital-asset investment company. — CoinShares publishes 2024 Annual Report

Financial news / other:

  • Feb. 18, 2025, Q4 2024 results release: CoinShares said its Physical platform’s assets rose to $2.3 billion and that its Physical Bitcoin ETP had become Europe’s largest, reinforcing direct crypto financial-services exposure. — CoinShares Q4 2024 results
  • Nov. 17, 2022, Reuters pickup: CoinShares disclosed $30.3 million of exposure to FTX including cash, USDC, and other assets, showing direct participation in crypto-market financial plumbing rather than merely peripheral service provision. — Reuters / Investing.com item on FTX exposure

SILVERGATE CAPITAL A

  • Rank: 24 | Expo: 8.00 | id_str: US82837P4081
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 1.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 2 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: earnings call transcripts (2021–2022), 2021 Annual Report / 10-K, 2023 press releases and policy reviews

Firm Overview

Silvergate Capital Corp was the holding company for Silvergate Bank, a U.S. bank that built a specialty franchise serving digital-asset market participants. Within the sample period, its crypto exposure came through banking and payments infrastructure, bitcoin-collateralized lending, and related services for exchanges, investors, stablecoin projects, and other digital-asset clients. (silvergate.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence points overwhelmingly to finserv. Management repeatedly described Silvergate as serving digital-asset customers through deposit accounts, the Silvergate Exchange Network (SEN), bitcoin-collateralized SEN Leverage loans, API wires, and stablecoin infrastructure initiatives, all of which are direct crypto financial services rather than peripheral support. (silvergate.com)

No evidence supports miners or holders: the bank explicitly stated that in SEN Leverage, bitcoin served as collateral held via custodians and that the bank did not directly hold pledged bitcoin, so this is not treasury crypto exposure. (silvergate.com) The case for infrastr is weaker than finserv because SEN and stablecoin rails were monetized as banking/payment products offered directly to crypto clients, not as general-purpose compute, hardware, or hosting infrastructure under the project’s infrastructure definition. (silvergate.com)

Weight Assignment

A full 1.0 finserv weight is assigned because the record across transcripts and filings is concentrated in crypto-focused banking, payments, lending, and transaction services. The company’s crypto relevance arose from handling fiat rails and lending for digital-asset participants, not from mining, treasury holding, non-financial consulting, or physical/compute infrastructure. invbase = 1 because outside coverage and official disclosures repeatedly framed Silvergate as a crypto-focused or “go-to crypto bank,” and the stock’s market narrative was tightly linked to crypto sentiment and the FTX-driven crisis. (silvergate.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main boundary issue is whether SEN and stablecoin technology could justify some infrastr weight. That weight is kept at zero because the company’s own descriptions frame these offerings as banking, payments, deposit, and lending solutions for digital-asset customers, which fit finserv more closely than standalone infrastructure provision. A second limitation is that Silvergate did not file a normal 2022 10-K on time and later moved into liquidation, so the filing base is strongest for 2021 plus 2022 earnings materials and 2023 current reports. (silvergate.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Jul. 20, 2021, Q2 2021 earnings call transcript: management said Silvergate was partnering with Diem “to become the exclusive issuer of U.S. dollar backed stablecoins known as Diem USD,” anchoring direct crypto financial-services activity. — Silvergate Q2 2021 transcript
  • Jul. 20, 2021, Q2 2021 earnings call transcript: management said Silvergate was “the transactional bank for U.S. regulated stablecoins,” tying the business to crypto payment rails and transaction services. — Silvergate Q2 2021 transcript
  • Apr. 19, 2022, Q1 2022 earnings call transcript: CEO Alan Lane said SEN transfer volume reached $142 billion and “transaction revenue from digital currency customers” was $8.9 million. — Silvergate Q1 2022 transcript
  • Apr. 19, 2022, Q1 2022 earnings call transcript: management described SEN Leverage as “our bitcoin collateralized lending product” and cited a $205 million loan to MacroStrategy. — Silvergate Q1 2022 transcript
  • Jan. 17, 2023, Q4 2022 earnings call transcript: management said SEN transfer volume was $117 billion, “demonstrating that our platform continues to serve as critical market infrastructure for the digital asset industry.” — Silvergate Q4 2022 transcript
  • Jan. 17, 2023, Q4 2022 earnings call transcript: management said its remaining offerings were “the SEN, wires via our proprietary API and deposit accounts,” confirming a crypto-focused financial-services model. — Silvergate Q4 2022 transcript

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Feb. 28, 2022, 2021 Annual Report / 10-K: Silvergate said it was the “leading provider of innovative financial infrastructure solutions and services to participants in the nascent and expanding digital currency industry.” — Silvergate 2021 Annual Report
  • Feb. 28, 2022, 2021 Annual Report / 10-K: the filing showed 1,381 digital currency customers with $14.111 billion of deposits and fee income from digital currency customers rising to $35.8 million in 2021. — Silvergate 2021 Annual Report
  • Feb. 28, 2022, 2021 Annual Report / 10-K: Silvergate said SEN enables U.S. dollar transfers among digital-currency participants 24/7 and called it “a first-of-its-kind digital currency infrastructure solution.” — Silvergate 2021 Annual Report
  • Feb. 28, 2022, 2021 Annual Report / 10-K: the company disclosed SEN Leverage loans collateralized by bitcoin, with $335.9 million outstanding at December 31, 2021, and stated that “At no time does the Bank directly hold the pledged bitcoin digital currency.” — Silvergate 2021 Annual Report
  • Mar. 20, 2023, 8-K: Silvergate warned about filing delays, going-concern issues, and referenced its prior 2021 10-K and other SEC filings amid the post-FTX fallout. — Silvergate March 20, 2023 8-K
  • Mar. 8, 2023, company press release: Silvergate announced voluntary liquidation of the bank, confirming the collapse of its crypto-focused banking model after industry stress. — Silvergate March 8, 2023 release

Financial news / other:

  • Mar. 9, 2023, CNBC: Silvergate was described as the “go-to crypto bank” when it announced liquidation, supporting invbase = 1 and the market’s treatment of the stock as a crypto proxy. — CNBC on Silvergate liquidation
  • Jan. 5, 2023, Forbes: coverage described Silvergate as a “cryptocurrency-focused financial institution,” with the stock moving sharply on crypto-related deposit withdrawals. — Forbes on Silvergate stock and crypto crisis
  • Sept. 27, 2023, Federal Reserve OIG review: the bank’s liquidation was tied to concentration in crypto-industry deposit customers and related funding risks. — Federal Reserve OIG review of Silvergate supervision
  • Nov. 2024, Congressional Research Service report: Silvergate’s last reporting period showed roughly 90% of deposits tied to crypto clients, underscoring the material dependence of the business on crypto financial services. — CRS report on banking and cryptocurrency

IREN

  • Rank: 25 | Expo: 7.97 | id_str: AU0000185993
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 1.0, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 4 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: earnings call transcripts (Q2 FY24, Q4 FY24), SEC filings / annual report materials (FY24, Q1 FY25), financial news

Firm Overview

IREN, formerly Iris Energy Ltd, is an Australia-founded Nasdaq-listed operator of data centers whose core business in the sample period was industrial-scale Bitcoin mining, with AI cloud services added as a secondary line by 2024. Across company materials, management consistently described Bitcoin mining as the principal earnings engine and framed the firm’s facilities as purpose-built for high-power compute workloads.

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports a pure miners classification. In the February 15, 2024 earnings call, management said Bitcoin mining is “using computing power to secure the network” and that the company “liquidate[s] those rewards directly into cash,” which fits the mining mechanism and excludes a treasury-holder classification. The September 30, 2024 Form 6-K likewise states that the company “primarily derive[s] our revenues from Bitcoin mining,” generated $49.6 million of quarterly Bitcoin mining revenue from mining and selling 813 Bitcoin, and “did not have any Bitcoin held on our balance sheet” as of September 30, 2024. Those disclosures rule out holders and also do not support finserv or bizserv. (reportify-1252068037.cos.ap-beijing.myqcloud.com)

Weight Assignment

A full 1.0 weight is assigned to miners because the firm explicitly operates mining rigs, reports mining revenue, operating hashrate, machine efficiency, and Bitcoin production, and repeatedly presents Bitcoin mining as its core business. No weight is assigned to holders because management says mined Bitcoin is typically liquidated daily and the company reported no Bitcoin on balance sheet at September 30, 2024. No infrastr weight is added even though IREN owns and expands data-center capacity, because in this sample the infrastructure is principally used for its own mining operations rather than sold as crypto-specific third-party infrastructure service. (reportify-1252068037.cos.ap-beijing.myqcloud.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main boundary issue is whether IREN’s data-center buildout and energy-management systems justify some infrastr weight. That weight is kept at zero because the sources reviewed describe those assets mainly as internal operating infrastructure supporting IREN’s own Bitcoin mining and, later, AI cloud activities, not as a standalone crypto infrastructure service sold to the digital-asset ecosystem. A second limitation is that transcript access was patchy through primary sites, so one transcript quote is anchored using a scraped PDF mirror; however, the classification is strongly corroborated by SEC-filed company disclosures.

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Feb. 15, 2024, Q2 FY24 earnings call transcript: CEO Daniel Roberts said, “Today, Bitcoin mining… It’s essentially using computing power to secure the network… We liquidate those rewards directly into cash,” directly anchoring the miner classification and excluding treasury holding. — Iris Energy Q2 FY24 transcript
  • Feb. 15, 2024, Q2 FY24 earnings call transcript: management said it was “on track to hit 20 exahash” and expected to become “one of the largest Bitcoin mining businesses on a standalone basis.” — Iris Energy Q2 FY24 transcript
  • Feb. 15, 2024, Q2 FY24 earnings call transcript: management linked the business to “2 macro tailwinds… around Bitcoin as a store of value” and AI, showing bitcoin-price sensitivity but still through a mining channel rather than financial intermediation. — Iris Energy Q2 FY24 transcript
  • Aug. 28, 2024, Q4 FY24 earnings call transcript summary: the FY24 call highlighted Bitcoin mining revenue rising to $184.1 million, 4,191 bitcoin mined, and mining capacity expansion as the core business driver. — Iris Energy Q4 FY24 transcript page

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Aug. 28, 2024, FY24 results / SEC-filed press release: Bitcoin mining revenue was $184.1 million for FY24 versus AI Cloud Service revenue of $3.1 million, indicating crypto mining was the dominant revenue line. — FY24 results press release filed with SEC
  • Nov. 26, 2024, Form 6-K for Q1 FY25: the company stated, “We primarily derive our revenues from Bitcoin mining,” and that it “currently liquidate[s] rewards that we earn from mining Bitcoin… typically on a daily basis.” — IREN Q1 FY25 Form 6-K
  • Nov. 26, 2024, Form 6-K for Q1 FY25: IREN disclosed, “we did not have any Bitcoin held on our balance sheet as of September 30, 2024,” excluding a non-zero holders weight. — IREN Q1 FY25 Form 6-K
  • Nov. 26, 2024, Form 6-K for Q1 FY25: Bitcoin mining revenue was $49.6 million for the quarter, generated from “the mining and sale of 813… Bitcoin.” — IREN Q1 FY25 Form 6-K

Financial news / other:

  • Jul. 10, 2024, CoinDesk citing Bernstein: Bernstein initiated coverage on miner Iris Energy, explicitly treating it as a Bitcoin miner whose outlook was tied to bitcoin and mining economics. — CoinDesk on Bernstein coverage of Iris Energy
  • 2024 investor materials page: the company’s investor events archive lists FY24 and interim results calls centered on Bitcoin mining capacity, mined bitcoin, and related operating metrics. — IREN investor events
  • Jan. 7, 2024, monthly investor update: the company described itself as a “leading owner and operator of institutional-grade, highly efficient proprietary Bitcoin mining data centers,” reinforcing market treatment of the stock as a crypto-sensitive mining name. — January 2024 monthly investor update release

GMO INTERNET GROUP

  • Rank: 26 | Expo: 7.74 | id_str: JP3152750000
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.3, finserv 0.7, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 2 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: earnings-call transcripts (2018, 2022, 2024), company filings / investor disclosures, company IR FAQ, financial news

Firm Overview

GMO Internet Group is a Japanese listed internet conglomerate spanning internet infrastructure, online finance, payments, and related digital services. Within that broader portfolio, its crypto exposure over 2017–2024 came through a dedicated cryptoasset business that explicitly included exchange, mining, and payment/stablecoin activities, with the listed group repeatedly highlighting GMO Coin as the main current earnings driver in crypto. (ir.gmo.jp)

Exposure Channels Identified

The firm fits finserv because management and investor materials explicitly describe a cryptoasset business including exchange and payment activities, and 2024–2025 transcripts specifically state that trading value and revenue in the exchange business developed by GMO Coin doubled year over year and returned to profitability. It also fits miners because GMO announced a Bitcoin mining business in September 2017, repeatedly described mining as one pillar of the cryptoasset segment, and disclosed mined Bitcoin/BCH output and hash rate in 2018. (ir.gmo.jp)

No holders weight is assigned because the evidence reviewed points to mining operations and customer-facing financial services rather than a deliberate treasury-HODL policy by the parent. No infrastr weight is assigned because although GMO at one point developed mining machines and cloud mining, the sources frame those as part of its own crypto business lines rather than a distinct third-party crypto infrastructure provider during the sample classification window. (ir.gmo.jp)

Weight Assignment

A 0.7 weight is assigned to finserv and 0.3 to miners. The heavier finserv weight reflects the stronger and more persistent evidence in later-period transcripts and presentations that the exchange business run by GMO Coin was the active, profitable, and emphasized crypto channel by 2022–2024, while mining remained part of the cryptoasset segment but appears less central in the later sample than in the 2017–2018 buildout phase. The weights therefore reflect both economic emphasis and management attention across the period, not merely the count of business lines. (ir.gmo.jp)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main boundary issue is whether the payment/stablecoin activity should increase the finserv weight further or whether early mining-machine development should trigger infrastr. I keep infrastr at zero because the reviewed evidence frames mining hardware/cloud mining as embedded within GMO’s own crypto business strategy rather than a clearly separable listed-group infrastructure franchise. Asset-share precision is limited because the retrieved materials show customer assets under custody and business activity, but do not clearly evidence parent-level crypto assets dominating the consolidated balance sheet. (ir.gmo.jp)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • May 10, 2018, Q1 2018 presentation/transcript: management said the “Cryptocurrency Segment” included “ii) Cryptocurrency Exchange Business” and “iii) Cryptocurrency Mining Business,” and aimed to become “No. 1 in the field of cryptocurrency.” — GMO FY2018 Q1 presentation/transcript
  • May 10, 2018, Q1 2018 presentation/transcript: GMO described the mining plan as “In-house mining,” “Develop, manufacture, and sell mining machines,” and “Cloud mining.” — GMO FY2018 Q1 presentation/transcript
  • Feb. 13, 2023, FY2022 financial results briefing transcript: management said, “The crypto asset business consists of the following three categories, mining, exchange, and payment.” — GMO FY2022 results transcript
  • Feb. 12, 2025, FY2024 financial results briefing transcript covering fiscal 2024: management said, “both trading value and revenue doubled YoY in the exchange business developed by GMO COIN,” and that operating profit returned to profitability. — GMO FY2024 results transcript
  • Aug. 8, 2024, Q2 2024 earnings-call transcript: management said “market is recovering in exchange business, which is operated by GMO Coin,” and that “number of accounts at GMO Coin is steadily growing.” — Alpha Spread Q2 2024 transcript page

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Sept. 7, 2017, company disclosure: GMO announced “Launching a New Business (Bitcoin Mining Business)” and said it had already “started cryptocurrency exchange and trading service.” — GMO disclosure on launch of Bitcoin mining business
  • Nov. 5, 2018, monthly mining disclosure: GMO said, “We are currently mining Bitcoin … and Bitcoin Cash,” and reported monthly mining reward and hash-rate data. — GMO monthly mining report
  • Nov. 12, 2024, FY2024 Q3 presentation: the “Cryptoasset Business” slide listed “Mining Business,” “Exchange business,” and “Payment business ┗ Issuing Stablecoin.” — GMO FY2024 Q3 presentation
  • Investor relations FAQ, accessed 2026: the company states, “In the cyptoasset (cryprocurrency) business, we operate mining business and crypto asset trading bussiness.” — GMO IR FAQ

Financial news / other:

  • Dec. 26, 2018, CoinDesk: GMO said it would stop making and selling mining machines after the crypto downturn but would continue in-house mining operations, confirming both mining exposure and the later narrowing of that activity. — CoinDesk on GMO mining machine exit
  • Sept. 13, 2022, CoinDesk: Anchorage custody support for GYEN was described as a partnership with GMO-Z.com Trust Company, a subsidiary of GMO Internet Group, corroborating payment/stablecoin-related crypto financial services within the group. — CoinDesk on GYEN custody partnership

BITFARMS

  • Rank: 27 | Expo: 7.61 | id_str: CA09173B1076
  • Weight summary: holders 0.1, miners 0.8, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.1, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 5 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: 2021–2023 annual filings/40-F, Q2 2024 and Q3 2024 prepared remarks, 2024 financial news on Riot’s bid

Firm Overview

Bitfarms Ltd. is a Canadian-listed company founded in 2017 that operates vertically integrated Bitcoin mining facilities across the Americas. Across the 2017–2024 sample, the retrieved filings and earnings-call materials consistently describe the firm primarily as a Bitcoin miner, while late-2024 materials also show an emerging shift toward energy, hosting, and HPC/AI-oriented infrastructure. (investor.bitfarms.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

Bitfarms fits miners unambiguously. Company materials repeatedly state that it contributes computational power to mining pools, receives payment in Bitcoin, reports BTC earned, mining revenues, direct cost per BTC, hashrate growth, and miner deployments. (investor.bitfarms.com)

A small holders weight is also warranted because the filings show material BTC on the balance sheet at year-end 2021 and 2022, including BTC purchased in 2022 rather than only mined coin awaiting sale. However, the evidence does not support a pure treasury-HODL identity on the scale of a dedicated crypto holder; the holdings appear secondary to mining operations and liquidity management. (investor.bitfarms.com)

A small infrastr weight is appropriate at the margin for late-2024 activity. Management discussed U.S. power assets, miner hosting agreements, and opportunities in energy generation, trading, and HPC/AI; this indicates an emerging crypto/digital infrastructure channel, but during the sample window it remained clearly subordinate to self-mining. (investor.bitfarms.com)

Weight Assignment

A weight of 0.8 miners is assigned because mining is the dominant and persistent exposure channel across filings and calls throughout the sample: 2022 mining revenue was $138.985 million out of $142.428 million total revenue, and in Q2 2024 mining revenue was $89.806 million out of $91.865 million total revenue. 0.1 holders reflects recurring BTC on balance sheet plus a documented 2022 BTC purchase, but not a management-led treasury accumulation story. 0.1 infrastr captures the late-sample move into hosting and power/HPC-linked infrastructure opportunities without overstating a business line that was still nascent by December 2024. (investor.bitfarms.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main boundary issue is whether BTC retained from mining should count as holders. Under the project rules, mined BTC held temporarily is mining exposure, so the holder weight is kept small and is justified mainly by the explicit 2022 BTC purchase and recurring balance-sheet digital assets rather than inventory from operations alone. A second boundary issue is whether the 2024 U.S. pivot and hosting/HPC discussion merits a larger infrastr weight; I keep it modest because the retrieved 2024 remarks still frame these assets as future monetization avenues around a business that remained predominantly Bitcoin mining during the sample window. (investor.bitfarms.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Aug. 8, 2024, Q2 2024 prepared remarks: management said, “The HPC/AI opportunity is a very exciting one,” but added that the company’s energy assets were “currently monetized through Bitcoin mining,” confirming mining as the incumbent core activity. — Bitfarms Q2 2024 Prepared Remarks
  • Aug. 8, 2024, Q2 2024 prepared remarks: Sharon in PJM offered power attractive for “Bitcoin mining, energy trading and even HPC/AI.” — Bitfarms Q2 2024 Prepared Remarks
  • Nov. 13, 2024, Q3 2024 prepared remarks: management said the U.S. assets were opening opportunities “beyond Bitcoin mining including energy generation, trading and HPC/AI.” — Bitfarms Q3 2024 Prepared Remarks

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Mar. 21, 2023, 40-F for FY2022: Note 9 showed “BTC mined” of 5,167 BTC valued at $138.985 million, “BTC purchased” of 1,000 BTC valued at $43.237 million, and year-end digital assets excluding pledged collateral of $4.635 million. — Bitfarms FY2022 Form 40-F
  • Mar. 21, 2023, 40-F for FY2022: total assets were $343.098 million at Dec. 31, 2022, implying digital assets were present but not balance-sheet dominant. — Bitfarms FY2022 Form 40-F
  • Mar. 21, 2023, 40-F for FY2022: 2022 mining revenues were $138.985 million versus non-mining revenues of $3.443 million. — Bitfarms FY2022 Form 40-F
  • Mar. 7, 2024, FY2023 results: Bitfarms reported 4,928 BTC earned in 2023. — Bitfarms FY2023 Results
  • Aug. 8, 2024, Q2 results: mining revenues were $89.806 million out of $91.865 million total six-month revenue, with 1,557 BTC earned. — Bitfarms Q2 2024 Results
  • Sept. 13, 2024, company release: Bitfarms entered a 10,000 miner hosting agreement with Stronghold, adding a direct hosting/infrastructure element. — Bitfarms Hosting Agreement Release
  • June 13, 2024, company release: Bitfarms described itself as a “global Bitcoin mining company” operating vertically integrated mining farms. — Bitfarms U.S. Development Release

Financial news / other:

  • May 28, 2024, Riot proposal: Riot described the combination as creating the world’s largest publicly listed Bitcoin miner, reinforcing how the market characterized Bitfarms. — Business Wire on Riot proposal
  • May 29, 2024, CoinDesk: Riot had acquired a 9.25% stake in Bitfarms in a takeover effort, corroborating strong crypto-linked investor attention around the stock. — CoinDesk on Riot’s takeover attempt
  • Sept. 23, 2024, company release: Bitfarms and Riot announced a settlement ahead of a special shareholder meeting, further evidencing crypto-specific shareholder dynamics. — Bitfarms and Riot Announce Settlement

BIT DIGITAL

  • Rank: 28 | Expo: 7.37 | id_str: KYG1144A1058
  • Weight summary: holders 0.2, miners 0.6, finserv 0.2, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 4 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: earnings releases / call remarks (2023–2024), SEC filings (2023 Form 20-F; 2024 Form 10-K), company press releases

Firm Overview

Bit Digital, Inc. is a Nasdaq-listed New York-headquartered company whose core business through most of 2017–2024 was bitcoin mining, with operations in North America and Iceland. During 2023–2024 it also built a meaningful Ethereum staking operation and, late in the sample, began diversifying into AI/HPC services and data-center activities. (sec.gov)

Exposure Channels Identified

The dominant channel is miners. Across filings and 2024 management commentary, Bit Digital repeatedly describes its operations around owning or operating bitcoin miners, reporting hash rate, BTC earned, mining electricity cost, and mining revenue. (bit-digital.com)

A secondary finserv weight is warranted because the company explicitly operated Ethereum staking activities from late 2022 onward, earned staking revenue, stated an intention to accumulate ETH and stake substantially all of its ETH position, and even formed a joint venture to explore digital-asset staking technology. That is direct crypto financial-service exposure rather than a mere treasury side note. (bit-digital.com)

A smaller holders weight is also appropriate. Bit Digital carried substantial BTC and ETH on balance sheet and discussed “treasury holdings,” but much of that inventory arose from mining and staking operations; under the project rules, operationally generated coin should not be overstated as standalone treasury exposure. The holder weight therefore reflects deliberate ETH accumulation language rather than simply coins temporarily retained from mining. (bit-digital.com)

Weight Assignment

A weight of 0.6 miners is assigned because mining remained the clearest and most persistent economic exposure through the sample: 2023 revenue was still overwhelmingly tied to digital asset mining, and even in 2024 bitcoin mining generated $58.6 million and 54% of total revenue. 0.2 finserv captures the explicit ETH staking business, staking revenues, staked ETH balances, and staking-technology initiative. 0.2 holders reflects management’s stated intention to accumulate ETH and the recurring presence of sizeable BTC/ETH treasury balances, while keeping this below mining because the firm’s coin holdings were closely intertwined with operating activities rather than a pure MicroStrategy-style treasury model. (sec.gov)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main boundary issue is between holders and finserv for ETH-related exposure. Because Bit Digital did more than passively hold ETH—it actively staked ETH, earned staking revenue, and described staking as a business—part of the exposure is classified as finserv rather than assigning all ETH-related evidence to holders. A second issue is whether late-2024 AI/HPC and colocation activity merits infrastr weight; I do not assign it within the 2017–2024 crypto taxonomy because those activities were not shown as materially crypto-directed infrastructure by December 31, 2024, and the firm’s crypto exposure remained centered on mining, staking, and digital-asset balances. (bit-digital.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Mar. 14, 2025, FY2024 results / management remarks covering FY2024: “Bitcoin mining remained a key revenue contributor, generating $58.6 million” while mining’s share of revenue declined as HPC scaled. — Bit Digital FY2024 Results
  • Aug. 2024, Q2 2024 management remarks: management said “Mining economics remain challenging” and that it continued evaluating the post-halving bitcoin mining landscape while favoring HPC for incremental capex. — Bit Digital Q2 2024 Results
  • May 2024, Q1 2024 management remarks: “We were well prepared for the halving” and the company said it continued to evaluate the post-halving bitcoin mining landscape while targeting 6.0 EH/s. — Bit Digital Q1 2024 Results

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Mar. 14, 2025, Form 10-K for FY2024: digital asset mining revenue was $58.6 million; total assets were $538.2 million. — Bit Digital FY2024 Form 10-K
  • Mar. 14, 2025, FY2024 results: revenue from digital asset mining comprised 54% of total 2024 revenue; BTC and ETH treasury holdings were worth about $69.3 million and $92.1 million, respectively, at Dec. 31, 2024. — Bit Digital FY2024 Results
  • Mar. 28, 2024, FY2023 results: the company earned 1,507.3 BTC in 2023, held 46,548 miners or operated miners with 3.9 EH/s maximum hash rate, and had 12,752 ETH actively staked at Dec. 31, 2023. — Bit Digital FY2023 Results
  • Mar. 28, 2024, Form 20-F for FY2023: operations were focused on bitcoin mining facilities in the U.S., Canada and Iceland, “as well as our Ethereum staking operations.” — Bit Digital FY2023 Form 20-F
  • Feb. 6, 2023, company release: Bit Digital said it had commenced Ethereum staking in late 2022, had 10,222 ETH plus 2,004 sETH-h, and “intends to continue accumulating Ethereum and stake substantially all of its ETH position over time.” — Bit Digital staking JV release

Financial news / other:

  • Mar. 20, 2024, Channelchek analyst note summarizing company disclosures: Bit Digital recognized about $44.2 million from bitcoin mining services and roughly $0.7 million from ETH staking in 2023, corroborating mining dominance with a smaller staking stream. — Channelchek on Bit Digital

BITDEER TECHNOLOGIES GROUP A

  • Rank: 29 | Expo: 6.67 | id_str: KYG114481008
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.6, finserv 0.3, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.1, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 5 | AssetShare: 1
