  • September 28, 2022, FY2022 earnings call transcript: management discussed scale, software/services growth, and immersive technology expansion, but no substantive crypto-financial activity was identified on the call. — Company-hosted transcript PDF

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • August 31, 2021, company release: Glimpse announced an “Enterprise Focused Virtual NFT and Crypto VR & AR Software Product Development Effort,” including a VR art gallery, AR NFT display system, and NFT asset-creation services. — Glimpse press release
  • September 28, 2023, FY2023 results release filed with the SEC: Glimpse said QReal completed a paid engagement for Sabrina Carpenter’s fragrance launch through “a virtual store and NFT experience in Decentraland,” supporting non-financial crypto-adjacent services revenue. — FY2023 results release
  • September 30, 2024, FY2024 Form 10-K: the company described itself as an immersive technology provider focused on VR, AR, and spatial computing software and services, with blockchain mentioned only as a broader emerging technology adjacency rather than a distinct operating segment. — FY2024 Form 10-K

Financial news / other:

  • January 2022 investor presentation: Glimpse listed “Blockchain/Crypto/NFT” among technology themes in its corporate materials, corroborating that management marketed blockchain-related capability during the sample period. — Investor presentation
  • Investor relations website, accessed March 13, 2026: current company positioning centers on immersive technology, spatial computing, AI, VR, and AR rather than crypto-specific products, reinforcing the peripheral and non-persistent nature of the crypto channel by the end of the sample. — Investor relations site

TASKUS A

  • Rank: 72 | Expo: 1.10 | id_str: US87652V1098
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.6, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.4
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: earnings call transcripts (2023–2024), Form 10-K filings (2024), company earnings releases

Firm Overview

TaskUs, Inc. is a U.S.-listed outsourced digital services company focused on digital customer experience, trust and safety, and AI services for technology-led clients globally. Within the sample period, crypto exposure appears through service provision to cryptocurrency and equity-trading clients rather than through direct ownership, trading, mining, custody, or dedicated blockchain infrastructure. (sec.gov)

Exposure Channels Identified

bizserv is the only clearly supported mechanism. Management stated on the August 9, 2023 Q2 2023 earnings call that results were affected by declines in volume at its “largest crypto, and equity trading clients,” indicating that TaskUs was servicing crypto-related customers as an outsourced operations provider rather than acting as a crypto financial intermediary itself. (fool.com)

The 2024 earnings call and 2024 Form 10-K corroborate that this exposure remained client-service based and relatively small. On the February 27, 2025 call covering Q4 2024, management said revenue from “crypto and equity trading clients” was about 4% of Q4 revenue, while the 2024 10-K grouped cryptocurrency within financial-services end markets and discussed volatility in demand from such clients, without any indication that TaskUs held digital assets, ran an exchange, or provided mining or crypto-specific infrastructure. (fool.com)

Weight Assignment

bizserv receives 0.6 because the evidence shows real, management-discussed revenue exposure tied to crypto clients, but the mechanism is indirect and service-based: TaskUs appears to provide outsourced customer experience, trust-and-safety, and related operational support to crypto firms rather than directly handling digital assets. unclass retains 0.4 because crypto is not presented as a standalone segment, remains a small minority of revenue by late 2024 at roughly 4% in Q4, and no evidence was found of direct crypto custody, treasury holdings, exchange activity, mining, or dedicated crypto infrastructure. RevShare is therefore coded 1 and AssetShare 0. (fool.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The evidence supports crypto-adjacent servicing of clients, but the precise nature of those services for crypto firms is not broken out separately in filings or transcripts beyond references to crypto and equity-trading clients within broader financial-services exposure. No evidence was found that TaskUs directly touched customer crypto assets, provided brokerage/custody/payment functions, or was treated by management or financial media as a crypto-proxy stock, so finserv, holders, miners, infrastr, and invbase remain at zero. None identified beyond the limited granularity of segment disclosure. (sec.gov)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • August 9, 2023, Q2 2023 earnings call transcript: management said results were impacted by declines in volume at its “largest crypto, and equity trading clients,” directly anchoring exposure to crypto-sector customers. — Motley Fool transcript
  • August 9, 2023, Q2 2023 earnings call transcript: management also said digital customer experience revenue was pressured by “the decline in revenues from crypto and equity trading clients,” reinforcing that the channel runs through outsourced services revenue. — Motley Fool transcript
  • February 27, 2025, Q4 2024 earnings call transcript: management said revenue from “crypto and equity trading clients was stable compared with Q3 and approximated 4% of total revenue during Q4,” showing crypto-linked revenue remained present but minor by the end of 2024. — Motley Fool transcript

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • March 3, 2025, FY2024 Form 10-K: TaskUs described itself as a provider of outsourced digital services, trust and safety, and AI services to clients across industries including financial services, not as a digital-asset operator. — FY2024 Form 10-K
  • March 3, 2025, FY2024 Form 10-K: risk-factor disclosure stated that “financial services (including cryptocurrency)” clients can experience substantial revenue variation, corroborating exposure to crypto-sector customers within a broader service business. — FY2024 Form 10-K
  • November 7, 2024, Q3 2024 results release: TaskUs highlighted broad-based growth across client verticals and delivery geographies but did not identify any direct crypto-asset activity, consistent with indirect client-service exposure only. — Q3 2024 results release

Financial news / other:

  • TaskUs investor relations materials, accessed March 13, 2026: current company positioning centers on outsourced digital operations, AI services, and customer experience rather than crypto products or infrastructure, reinforcing that crypto exposure is peripheral and client-driven. — TaskUs IR events and presentations
  • Company insight article, accessed March 13, 2026: TaskUs markets fraud and risk-management expertise relevant to financial-crime prevention, which is consistent with business-services support capabilities rather than direct crypto financial intermediation. — TaskUs insight article

VERSABANK

  • Rank: 73 | Expo: 1.10 | id_str: CA92512J1066
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 0.7, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.3, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: earnings call transcripts (Q1 2022, Q3 2022, Q4 2024), 2024 annual filings / MD&A / AIF

Firm Overview

VersaBank is a Canadian branchless digital bank listed on Nasdaq and the TSX under ticker VBNK, with core operations in digital banking and a technology subsidiary business. Within the 2017–2024 sample, its crypto-related activity centered on developing bank-issued blockchain-based Digital Deposit Receipts (DDRs) and related secure digital-vault technology rather than holding cryptocurrencies on its own balance sheet. (versabank.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

finserv is the primary mechanism because management repeatedly described DDRs as bank-issued digital deposits or tokenized deposits designed for transacting and mainstream financial applications. On the March 2, 2022 Q1 2022 call, management said DDRs were “a one-for-one representation of a fiat currency on deposit” and “the ideal digital currency,” while on the November 29, 2022 Q4 2022 call it said the DDR accounts were “essentially e-wallets.” (versabank.com)

infrastr also applies because VersaBank developed and marketed the underlying blockchain and custody stack, including VersaVault and affiliated technology subsidiaries, as infrastructure enabling digital-asset use by banks and security-conscious clients. The 2024 interim financial statements described Digital Meteor as owning IP to enable “the next generation of digital assets” including DDRs, and described VersaVault as a digital vault for sensitive material and “blockchain-based assets.” (versabank.com)

Weight Assignment

finserv receives 0.7 because the strongest evidence points to VersaBank functioning as a regulated financial intermediary designing bank-issued digital deposit instruments for payments and financial applications, which fits crypto financial services more closely than any other category. infrastr receives 0.3 because the same business also includes proprietary enabling technology, blockchain issuance rails, and secure custody-style software through VersaVault and Digital Meteor. holders, miners, and bizserv remain at zero: management explicitly said in the August 31, 2022 Q3 2022 call, “We hold no such assets. We never have,” referring to cryptocurrencies, and no evidence was found of mining activity or non-financial crypto services. RevShare is coded 1 because crypto/digital-asset activity was discussed as a strategic initiative and segment but was not evidenced as a major revenue driver by 2024; AssetShare is 0 because no material crypto assets were shown on balance sheet and management denied crypto holdings. (versabank.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main ambiguity is that VersaBank’s DDR initiative sat at the boundary between regulated tokenized deposits, stablecoin-like products, and enabling infrastructure, so some judgment is required between finserv and infrastr. The available filings and calls support both, but they do not provide a clean revenue breakout for DDR-related activity during 2017–2024, which limits precision on materiality. No direct evidence was found that financial media treated VBNK as a major crypto-proxy stock during the sample, so invbase remains 0. (versabank.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • March 2, 2022, Q1 2022 earnings call transcript: management said DDRs were “a one-for-one representation of a fiat currency on deposit” and “the ideal digital currency,” directly anchoring a tokenized-deposit / digital-currency financial-service model. — VersaBank Q1 2022 transcript
  • November 29, 2022, Q4 2022 earnings call transcript: management said its DDR accounts were “essentially e-wallets,” reinforcing direct financial-product functionality. — VersaBank Q4 2022 transcript
  • August 31, 2022, Q3 2022 earnings call transcript: management stated, “We hold no such assets. We never have,” clarifying that VersaBank was not a crypto treasury holder despite DDR development. — VersaBank Q3 2022 transcript
  • December 9, 2024, Q4 2024 earnings call transcript: management said its digital deposit receipts are on the “Algorand, Ethereum, and Stellar Blockchains” and paired them with VersaVault, supporting continued infrastructure and financial-product development. — VersaBank Q4 2024 transcript

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • December 2024, interim financial statements: VersaBank identified Digital Meteor as a reportable segment owning IP to enable digital assets including DDRs, and said DDRs are “highly encrypted digital assets” issued on Algorand, Ethereum, and Stellar. — Interim Consolidated Financial Statements
  • December 8, 2024, Annual Information Form: DRTC’s VersaVault was described as a digital vault for clients holding digital assets, supporting an infrastructure channel alongside banking applications. — 2024 Annual Information Form
  • 2024 Form 40-F filing package: the SEC filing incorporated VersaBank’s 2024 AIF, audited financials, and MD&A for the year ended October 31, 2024, corroborating the disclosure set used for classification. — 2024 Form 40-F
  • 2024 annual MD&A: total revenue remained driven by core banking results, with no evidence that DDR activity constituted a major standalone revenue stream by 2024. — 2024 MD&A

Financial news / other:

  • November 30, 2021, company media page citing PYMNTS coverage: VersaBank said it was expanding closed-ecosystem testing of DDRs to include the U.S. dollar plus Algorand and Ethereum blockchains. — VersaBank media page
  • January 29, 2025, PR Newswire release: VersaBank said it transferred DDR assets and resources to a wholly owned subsidiary focused on the digital-asset opportunity, consistent with dedicated infrastructure and product development. — PR Newswire release
  • VersaBank events page, accessed March 13, 2026: investor materials described the product as “The Only USD Digital Deposit Receipt,” reinforcing management’s framing of DDRs as a banking product rather than a crypto treasury investment. — VersaBank events & presentations

GB GROUP

  • Rank: 74 | Expo: 1.10 | id_str: GB0006870611
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 0.0, bizserv 1.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: earnings call transcripts (FY22, H1 FY23, FY24), annual reports (2022–2024)

Firm Overview

GB Group plc (“GBG”) is a UK-listed identity verification, location intelligence, and fraud-prevention software company serving digital businesses, financial institutions, and other enterprises globally. Within the 2017–2024 sample, the crypto-related activity evidenced in sources reflects GBG supplying identity and fraud tools to crypto-exchange customers rather than operating a crypto exchange, holding crypto, mining, or providing crypto-targeted infrastructure. (gbgplc.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports bizserv only. Management repeatedly discussed “crypto” as an end-market/customer vertical inside GBG’s Identity business, with revenue exposure tied to transaction volumes from crypto customers and crypto exchanges, indicating GBG sold verification/onboarding and fraud tools into the sector rather than directly handling digital assets itself. (gbgplc.com)

The exclusion rules point away from finserv because no source found GBG operating a crypto exchange, brokerage, custody platform, payments product, token issuance business, or investment product. The evidence also does not support holders, miners, or infrastr: annual reports and transcripts show no balance-sheet crypto holdings, mining operations, or purpose-built mining/blockchain infrastructure activity. (gbgplc.com)

Weight Assignment

bizserv receives 1.0 because the crypto linkage that appears in management commentary is entirely through selling non-financial identity/fraud software and services to crypto-sector clients. The strongest transcript evidence is management’s statement in the November 30, 2021 half-year call that “very strong crypto volumes” contributed about £4 million early in the period, followed by later calls showing that crypto customer revenue declined as market conditions weakened and by FY24 represented only about 1% of group revenue. That pattern indicates real but peripheral service-provider exposure, so RevShare is coded 1 and AssetShare is 0. (gbgplc.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main limitation is that the available sources describe crypto mostly as a customer end-market rather than as a separately reported operating segment, so exact crypto-attributable revenue is only partially disclosed. There is nonetheless enough explicit management commentary in transcripts to classify GBG confidently as a crypto-ecosystem service provider, not a direct crypto financial intermediary. No evidence was found that financial media treated GBG as a major crypto-proxy stock or that management discussed a crypto-driven investor base, so invbase remains 0. (gbgplc.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • November 30, 2021, Half Year Results transcript: management said there were “very strong crypto volumes, particularly at the beginning of the period, April and May, which accounted for about £4 million,” anchoring explicit crypto-customer exposure through services revenue. — GBG H1 FY22 transcript
  • November 29, 2022, Half Year FY23 transcript: management said the decline was driven by “two exceptional end-market issues: crypto and internet economy,” and added that crypto revenues were split out from financial services and had fallen from 6% of total revenue to 3%, showing customer-vertical exposure rather than direct crypto intermediation. — GBG H1 FY23 transcript
  • June 16, 2022, FY22 results transcript: management said it had seen “a significant boost to crypto” in the prior period, again describing crypto as a demand driver for GBG’s verification business. — GBG FY22 transcript
  • June 11, 2024, FY24 results transcript: management stated that “crypto exchange customers” were “a very small proportion of Group revenue at just 1%,” confirming continuing but minor exposure via customer mix. — GBG FY24 transcript

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Annual Report 2022: CEO letter said the company benefited from a “crypto currency market” tailwind that gave it a fast start to FY22, consistent with crypto-related customer demand rather than proprietary crypto activity. — GBG Annual Report 2022
  • Annual Report 2023: the report quantified a “Full YoY decline in crypto customer revenue” of £15.4 million in the Identity business bridge, supporting the view that crypto exposure sat in customer revenues for verification services. — GBG Annual Report 2023
  • Annual Report 2024: the report describes GBG as an identity, location and fraud software provider and contains no evidence of crypto holdings, mining, exchange operation, or digital-asset custody. — GBG Annual Report 2024

Financial news / other:

  • GBG investor resources page, accessed March 13, 2026: the company’s archived reports and transcripts for FY22–FY25 corroborate the source set used for classification across the sample period. — GBG reports and presentations
  • GBG corporate site, accessed March 13, 2026: GBG describes itself as an identity verification and fraud-prevention company, consistent with a non-financial service-provider role. — GBG corporate overview
  • Bloomberg company profile, accessed via search snippet: GB Group is described as developing identity verification, location intelligence, and fraud prevention software solutions, supporting the general company characterization used in the report. — Bloomberg company profile

VISA 'A'

  • Rank: 75 | Expo: 1.07 | id_str: US92826C8394
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 1.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: earnings call transcripts (2021–2023), annual report/10-K filings (2024), Visa investor press release (2023)

Firm Overview

Visa Inc. is a U.S.-listed global payments network operator that facilitates transactions among consumers, merchants, financial institutions, and governments worldwide. Within the sample period, its crypto exposure appeared not as treasury investing or mining, but through payment-network, card, settlement, and advisory services tied to crypto wallets, exchanges, stablecoins, and related clients.

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports finserv only. Management explicitly described Visa’s role in enabling consumers to buy crypto with Visa credentials, cash out from crypto wallets onto Visa credentials, issue Visa cards with crypto platforms, help financial institutions offer crypto trading and custody, and test/expand USDC-based settlement inside Visa’s treasury and settlement operations. (s1.q4cdn.com)

The exclusion rules point away from holders, miners, bizserv, and infrastr. No retrieved filing or transcript evidence showed strategic balance-sheet crypto accumulation, mining activity, or purpose-built mining/blockchain infrastructure sales; instead, Visa’s crypto activity was embedded in payments intermediation and related financial services. The 2024 annual report mentions cryptocurrencies, blockchain, and stablecoin-based payments mainly in the context of competition and technological change rather than proprietary crypto asset holdings. (sec.gov)

Weight Assignment

finserv receives 1.0 because the crypto linkage is consistently described as direct payments and financial intermediation activity: Visa-linked wallet cards, crypto purchase and cash-out flows, support for financial institutions offering crypto capabilities, and live USDC settlement with issuer and acquirer partners. The transcript record is strong and repeated across years, while the evidence for other mechanism categories is absent. Crypto appears economically real but still peripheral to Visa’s much larger overall payments business, so RevShare is coded 1 and AssetShare is coded 0. (s1.q4cdn.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main limitation is that Visa does not separately report a crypto revenue segment in the retrieved filings, so the exact revenue share must be inferred as minor from management framing and scale disclosures rather than from a clean segment line. A further judgment call is that Visa’s crypto advisory practice could look adjacent to bizserv, but the stronger and more material evidence is that Visa directly handles payment, credential, settlement, and custody-enablement flows involving digital assets, which fits finserv under the project rules. No evidence was found that Visa stock was primarily treated as a crypto-proxy equity or that management described a crypto-driven shareholder base, so invbase remains 0. (fool.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • January 28, 2021, Q1 2021 earnings call transcript: management said Visa’s strategy was to work with wallets and exchanges to let users purchase these currencies using their Visa credentials or to cash out and added that “35 of the leading digital currency platforms and wallets have already chosen to issue Visa.” — FactSet corrected Q1 2021 transcript
  • January 27, 2022, Q1 2022 earnings call transcript: management said Visa had launched a “specialized global crypto advisory practice” to help financial institutions offer crypto solutions, retailers explore NFTs, and central banks explore digital currencies. — Motley Fool Q1 2022 transcript
  • March 8, 2022, RBC Capital Markets conference transcript: management said Visa had “partnered with over 60 crypto stablecoin providers” and was enabling institutions to “buy, trade in custody crypto” while also “working on settling in crypto… stablecoins.” — FactSet RBC conference transcript
  • April 25, 2023, Q2 2023 earnings call transcript: management said blockchain and crypto were “still early days” but highlighted ongoing work enabling on-chain and stablecoin-related capabilities, consistent with continuing direct financial-services exposure. — FactSet Q2 2023 transcript
  • November 17, 2022, MoffettNathanson Cryptocurrency & Blockchain Immersion transcript: Visa’s VP-Crypto discussed work with Coinbase and Crypto.com on issuer and merchant programs, crypto rewards cards, stablecoin off-ramps, and interoperability use cases. — FactSet MoffettNathanson transcript

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • Annual Report 2024: Visa’s risk and competition discussion cites “cryptocurrency or stablecoin-based payments initiatives” and notes technological change around “cryptocurrencies, distributed ledger and blockchain technologies,” corroborating that digital-asset payment activity is relevant to its business environment. — Visa Annual Report 2024
  • September 5, 2023, company press release: Visa said it had “already moved millions of USDC” over Solana and Ethereum to settle fiat-denominated payments and described Crypto.com using USDC to fulfill settlement obligations on a live Visa card program. — Visa stablecoin settlement announcement

Financial news / other:

  • January 28, 2022, CNBC: reported Visa disclosed $2.5 billion of payments volume on crypto-linked cards in its fiscal first quarter of 2022, supporting the view that crypto activity was real but still small relative to Visa’s total scale. — CNBC report
  • Visa annual report site, accessed March 13, 2026: fiscal 2024 net revenue was $35.9 billion, providing scale context for coding crypto-related revenue exposure as minor rather than core. — Visa annual report financials

MASTERCARD

  • Rank: 76 | Expo: 1.06 | id_str: US57636Q1040
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 1.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: earnings call transcripts (2020–2024), 2024 Form 10-K, company press releases, CNBC/Reuters coverage

Firm Overview

Mastercard Incorporated is a global payments network and financial technology company listed in the United States. Across the 2017–2024 sample, its crypto exposure appears in payment enablement, card programs, blockchain-based settlement, and compliance tools tied to digital-asset activity rather than in proprietary crypto treasury holdings or mining.

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports finserv only. Management repeatedly described Mastercard as enabling consumers to buy crypto on card, spend crypto-linked balances across Mastercard’s acceptance network, cash out proceeds via Mastercard Send, and support stablecoin or CBDC-related payment flows, which is direct crypto financial intermediation rather than peripheral consulting. (fool.com)

The firm also discussed blockchain infrastructure such as the Multi-Token Network and acquired crypto-risk capabilities through CipherTrace, but these were framed as part of Mastercard’s payments, settlement, compliance, and network services stack rather than as stand-alone infrastructure sold primarily to miners or blockchain operators. Under the project rules, the stronger and more material mechanism is therefore finserv, not bizserv or infrastr. (fool.com)

No evidence was found that Mastercard held crypto as a treasury asset, mined digital assets, or was primarily treated by management as a crypto-proxy stock with a crypto-driven shareholder base. (investor.mastercard.com)

Weight Assignment

finserv receives 1.0 because the transcript record is consistent across multiple years and explicitly ties Mastercard to crypto card issuance, crypto purchase and spend functionality, crypto cash-out, stablecoin settlement readiness, and blockchain-based payment rails. The activity is clearly real and recurring, but filings do not disclose a separate crypto revenue segment and the scale of Mastercard’s overall business remains overwhelmingly broader than crypto, so RevShare is coded 1 and AssetShare 0. (fool.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main limitation is measurement, not mechanism. Mastercard discusses crypto and blockchain regularly, but the retrieved filings do not provide a clean stand-alone crypto revenue line, so the revenue-share code must be inferred as minor relative to total company revenue. A secondary borderline issue is whether products such as CipherTrace or MTN could justify some bizserv or infrastr weight, but the evidence more strongly shows these capabilities embedded in Mastercard’s direct payments, settlement, and compliance offerings around digital assets, which fits finserv best. No explicit evidence was found of a crypto-proxy investor base, so invbase remains 0. (cnbc.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • January 28, 2021, Q4 2020 earnings call transcript: management said Mastercard’s “cryptocard programs, including Wirex in the U.K. and Uphold in the U.S., enable consumers to spend their crypto balances within our acceptance network.” — Motley Fool Q4 2020 transcript
  • April 29, 2021, Q1 2021 earnings call transcript: management said Mastercard had “several new crypto partnerships,” including Gemini rewards cards, and was “innovating in this space through new crypto and CBDC partnerships, enabling digital currencies on our network.” — Motley Fool Q1 2021 transcript
  • July 29, 2021, Q2 2021 earnings call transcript: management said Mastercard wanted to play a role across CBDCs, private-sector stablecoins and floating cryptos, and was getting its network ready to carry stablecoins as settlement currencies if criteria were met. — Motley Fool Q2 2021 transcript
  • October 28, 2021, Q3 2021 earnings call transcript: management said Mastercard’s crypto program allows consumers to “buy crypto assets with their Mastercard, spend their crypto balances wherever Mastercard is accepted, cash out their proceeds with Mastercard Send and earn rewards in the form of crypto or even NFT.” — Motley Fool Q3 2021 transcript
  • July 27, 2023, Q2 2023 earnings call transcript: management said it was introducing the Mastercard Multi-Token Network, “a set of foundational capabilities designed to make transactions within digital asset and blockchain ecosystems more secure, scalable, and interoperable.” — Motley Fool Q2 2023 transcript
  • January 30, 2025, Q4 2024 earnings call transcript discussing fiscal 2024: management said Mastercard was “partnering with a wide range of crypto players” to let consumers buy cryptocurrencies on card and spend crypto balances, and described MTN as a payment settlement solution for cross-border B2B flows. — Motley Fool Q4 2024 transcript

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • February 12, 2025, 2024 Form 10-K: Mastercard said it is “focused on supporting blockchain ecosystems and digital currencies,” uses MTN to enable programmable payments, and works with “a wide range of crypto players” so consumers can buy cryptocurrencies on card and spend balances where Mastercard is accepted. — Mastercard 2024 Form 10-K
  • July 20, 2021, company press release: Mastercard announced a simplified card offering for cryptocurrency companies, with partners including Paxos and Circle, to let banks and crypto firms offer cards that spend digital assets anywhere Mastercard is accepted. — Mastercard Newsroom press release
  • May 29, 2024, company press release: Mastercard said its Crypto Credential went live for peer-to-peer pilot transactions, allowing exchange users to send and receive crypto using aliases instead of blockchain addresses. — Mastercard Crypto Credential press release

Financial news / other:

  • April 13, 2022, Reuters: reported that Nexo and Mastercard launched what was described as a crypto-backed payment card, corroborating Mastercard’s active role in crypto-linked payment products. — Reuters via Investing.com
  • January 18, 2022, CNBC: reported Mastercard struck an NFT payments deal with Coinbase amid a broader series of crypto partnerships, supporting the view that management’s crypto strategy was commercially active rather than merely exploratory. — CNBC report
  • November 20, 2023, CNBC: reported Mastercard partnered with Feedzai to connect to CipherTrace Armada for monitoring transactions from thousands of crypto exchanges for fraud and AML risk, reinforcing that Mastercard directly services crypto transaction flows. — CNBC report

OXBRIDGE RE HOLDINGS

  • Rank: 77 | Expo: 1.04 | id_str: KYG6856M1069
  • Weight summary: holders 0.2, miners 0.0, finserv 0.8, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: 2023–2024 Form 10-K filings, Q4 2024 earnings call transcript, company press releases

Firm Overview

Oxbridge Re Holdings Ltd is a Cayman Islands-based Nasdaq-listed reinsurer focused on property and casualty reinsurance, primarily through its licensed reinsurance subsidiaries. During the sample period, it also developed a Web3-focused subsidiary, SurancePlus, to issue tokenized reinsurance securities and, by January 2025, adopted Bitcoin and Ethereum as treasury reserve assets. (nasdaq.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

The clearest crypto mechanism is finserv. Oxbridge, through SurancePlus, issues digital tokens and tokenized securities that give investors indirect fractional exposure to reinsurance contracts, meaning the firm is directly creating and distributing blockchain-based investment products rather than merely offering back-office services. (sec.gov)

A secondary holders channel is also present. In the Q4 2024 earnings call and a January 21, 2025 company press release, management said the board approved Bitcoin and Ethereum as treasury reserve assets, which is deliberate balance-sheet crypto exposure distinct from the tokenized reinsurance product. (investing.com)

No evidence was found that Oxbridge mines crypto, primarily sells hardware/compute to crypto users, or provides non-financial support services without touching the asset/product layer. The blockchain activity shown in filings and presentations is tied to issuance and distribution of tokenized securities, which fits finserv rather than bizserv or infrastr. (sec.gov)

Weight Assignment

finserv receives 0.8 because the most substantive and repeated evidence across filings, transcript material, and company materials concerns SurancePlus issuing tokenized reinsurance securities, raising capital from outside investors, and earning related fees/expenses around those offerings. holders receives 0.2 because treasury crypto adoption is explicit but appears newly approved near the end of the sample and is less central than the tokenized-investment-product activity. invbase remains 0 because no retrieved source explicitly described Oxbridge as a crypto-proxy stock or documented crypto-driven shareholder flows. (sec.gov)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main boundary issue is that Oxbridge’s tokenized products are tokenized reinsurance securities rather than conventional crypto exchange, brokerage, or custody services. They nonetheless fit finserv under the project rules because the firm directly issues and manages blockchain-based investment products. A second limitation is timing: the Bitcoin/Ethereum treasury decision was announced on January 21, 2025, just after the stated January 2017–December 2024 sample window, although it was discussed on the March 26, 2025 call as part of fiscal 2024 positioning; accordingly, holders is included but kept secondary. No pre-2025 earnings-call transcript with a stronger crypto quote was readily retrievable, so certainty is 3 rather than 4. (globenewswire.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • March 26, 2025, Q4 2024 earnings call transcript: management said, “This decision reflects our commitment to innovation, diversification and long term value creation... We believe Bitcoin in particular has demonstrated its strength as a resilient store of value.” — Investing.com Q4 2024 transcript
  • March 26, 2025, Q4 2024 earnings call transcript: management said the Plume partnership “expands the potential distribution channel of our tokenized reinsurance offering” and strengthens its role in the tokenization ecosystem. — ROIC AI Q4 2024 transcript

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • March 26, 2024, 2023 Form 10-K: SurancePlus “leverages blockchain technology to democratize access to high-return reinsurance contracts via digital securities.” — SEC 2023 Form 10-K
  • March 26, 2025, 2024 Form 10-K: the company said SurancePlus was formed for “tokenizing reinsurance contracts,” and reported 2024 issuance of EpsilonCat Re participation shares represented by digital tokens on the Avalanche blockchain, raising about $2.88 million. — SEC 2024 Form 10-K
  • January 21, 2025, company press release: Oxbridge announced that its board approved including Bitcoin and Ethereum as treasury reserve assets. — GlobeNewswire press release

Financial news / other:

  • March 20, 2025, Nasdaq/GlobeNewswire syndication: described Oxbridge as engaged in “tokenized Real-World Assets,” initially tokenized reinsurance securities, through SurancePlus. — Nasdaq article
  • January 21, 2025, Reinsurance Business: reported Oxbridge added Bitcoin and Ethereum to treasury reserves as part of a diversification strategy. — Reinsurance Business report
  • May 2025 corporate presentation: SurancePlus described itself as issuing tokenized reinsurance securities and noted that capital in reinsurance contracts is held in fully collateralized trust accounts in USD, clarifying that the core crypto exposure comes from security-token issuance rather than crypto-backed liabilities. — Oxbridge/SurancePlus corporate presentation

PAYSAFE

  • Rank: 78 | Expo: 1.00 | id_str: BMG6964L2062
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 1.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: 2021 F-1 / investor filing, 2025 20-F, Q4 2023 earnings call, company crypto-partner press release

Firm Overview

Paysafe Ltd is a UK-headquartered, NYSE-listed payments platform whose core businesses include merchant acquiring, digital wallets such as Skrill and NETELLER, and eCash solutions such as paysafecard. Within that broader payments stack, the firm has offered crypto-related wallet and payment services to consumers and digital-asset platforms, making crypto an adjacent but non-core part of its financial-services business. (ir.paysafe.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

The relevant channel is finserv. Paysafe explicitly states in its 2025 20-F that consumers can use its digital wallets “to buy, sell and withdraw digital assets,” and that certain subsidiaries are registered as Virtual Asset Service Providers for crypto-related services in the UK and Ireland. (ir.paysafe.com)

The transcript anchor is weaker on direct crypto detail than for some pure-play firms, but management in the November 2021 Q3 context described Paysafe as positioned to win in “high growth and disruptive markets including online sports betting and crypto,” indicating that crypto was an identified commercial vertical rather than a passing mention. (nasdaq.com)

Supporting company evidence shows Paysafe directly serving a cryptocurrency exchange through card processing, digital wallets, bank transfer, and eCash products, with management describing these as “on and off ramps.” That fits finserv because the firm is providing payment and wallet services directly tied to digital-asset transactions, not merely back-office consulting or generic infrastructure. (ir.paysafe.com)

Weight Assignment

finserv receives 1.0 because the evidence consistently points to direct crypto-related payment and wallet activity: regulated crypto services through Skrill/NETELLER, consumer ability to buy and sell digital assets, and commercial partnerships with crypto exchanges. No evidence was found of treasury crypto holdings, mining activity, or crypto-specific hardware/hosting exposure. invbase remains 0 because the retrieved sources did not explicitly describe Paysafe as a crypto-proxy stock or document crypto-driven shareholder flows. (ir.paysafe.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main limitation is materiality. Retrieved filings and transcript snippets confirm genuine crypto-related financial services, but they do not clearly quantify crypto as a separate reporting segment, so the activity appears ancillary to a much larger general payments platform. In addition, accessible earnings-call transcript evidence within the sample is more explicit about crypto as a growth vertical in 2021 than in later years, while later filings provide the clearest operational and regulatory detail; for that reason, certainty is 3 rather than 4. (nasdaq.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • November 11, 2021, Q3 2021 earnings context: management said, “Our position to win in high growth and disruptive markets including online sports betting and crypto continues to accelerate.” — Nasdaq summary citing Paysafe earnings remarks
  • March 7, 2024, Q4 2023 earnings call transcript: management discussed Digital Wallets growth and the wallet segment as a core product area, providing context for the channel through which crypto services are offered. — Fintool Q4 2023 transcript

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • May 19, 2021, F-1: Paysafe said its Digital Wallet segment includes Skrill and NETELLER, and described those wallets as enabling users to “send, spend, store and accept funds online” across its digital commerce network. — SEC F-1 / investor filing
  • March 4, 2025, 2024 20-F: the company stated, “Consumers can use our digital wallets to buy, sell and withdraw digital assets.” — SEC 2024 20-F
  • March 4, 2025, 2024 20-F: Paysafe said it is registered as a Virtual Asset Service Provider in the UK and that an Irish subsidiary is also registered for crypto-related services. — SEC 2024 20-F

Financial news / other:

  • May 4, 2023, company press release: CryptoOrange selected Paysafe as payments partner, using card processing, Skrill, NETELLER, Rapid Transfer, paysafecard, and SafetyPay for crypto exchange customers. — Paysafe press release
  • June 29, 2023, Reuters-syndicated report: Paysafe said it would cease offering its embedded wallet solution to Binance in the EEA, corroborating that it had been directly providing crypto-related payment/wallet services to a major exchange. — Investing.com / Reuters report

RF CAPITAL GROUP DEAD - DELIST.04/11/25

  • Rank: 79 | Expo: 0.98 | id_str: CA74971G4016
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 1.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: NA
  • RevShare: NA | AssetShare: NA
  • Primary sources: annual reports and shareholder reports (2021–2024), earnings-call transcript listings, company press releases

Firm Overview

RF Capital Group Inc. was a Canadian, TSX-listed wealth management company operating under the Richardson Wealth brand, focused on advisor-led wealth management, insurance, and related client-asset administration. Across the retrieved 2021–2024 annual and quarterly shareholder materials, the business is described as an independent wealth manager pursuing advisor support, recruiting, and digital platform modernization rather than crypto-related activity. Source name

Exposure Channels Identified

No substantive crypto exposure channel was identified in the sample period, so the firm is classified as unclass. The available company filings and shareholder reports discuss wealth management revenue, interest income, insurance, recruiting, advisor technology tools, and operational transformation, but no mining, treasury crypto holdings, crypto financial services, crypto-focused business services, or crypto-targeted infrastructure were evidenced. Source name

An earnings-call transcript anchor could be located only at the listing level rather than as a directly scraped transcript passage, and the retrieved accessible company materials within 2021–2024 produced no crypto-related mention to support a non-zero exposure weight. A 2024 appointment of a “Head of Digital Strategies” refers to internal digital transformation, data, AI, and advisor-service capabilities, not digital assets or crypto services. Source name Source name

Weight Assignment

unclass receives 1.0 because the evidence consistently describes RF Capital as a traditional wealth-management platform with no explicit crypto business line or balance-sheet exposure. The 2023 annual report contains no crypto, bitcoin, digital-asset, or cryptocurrency references in the retrieved text, and the 2024 quarterly shareholder reports likewise frame “digital” as internal technology and process modernization rather than crypto activity. invbase is 0 because no retrieved source described RF Capital as a crypto proxy or documented crypto-driven shareholder flows. Source name Source name Source name

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main limitation is transcript accessibility: transcript listings for RF Capital exist, but an accessible scraped earnings-call quote directly addressing crypto could not be retrieved, so there is no transcript quote to anchor a positive classification. There are isolated Richardson Wealth website items and advisor-blog content discussing “digital assets” or “crypto” at a general market-commentary level, but these appear to be generic advisory content rather than evidence that RF Capital itself operated a crypto business, held crypto on balance sheet, or offered direct crypto custody/trading/services at the corporate level; accordingly they were not used to assign exposure. Source name Source name Source name

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • Seeking Alpha transcript archive for RCG:CA shows multiple RF Capital earnings-call transcripts across 2017–2024, but no accessible crypto-related passage was retrievable from the available search results; this absence contributes to the unclassified outcome. — Seeking Alpha transcript listing

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • February 29, 2024, 2023 annual report: revenue is broken out across wealth management, corporate finance, interest, insurance, and other; no crypto, bitcoin, digital-asset, or cryptocurrency references were found in the retrieved report text. — RF Capital 2023 Annual Report
  • July 31, 2024, Q2 2024 report to shareholders: management described hiring Marcus Chun to lead “data and analytics, artificial intelligence and robotic process automation capabilities” for the business, indicating internal digital transformation rather than crypto activity. — RF Capital Q2 2024 Report to Shareholders
  • November 7, 2024, Q3 2024 report to shareholders: the firm’s strategy centered on advisor support, recruiting, and partnering with like-minded firms; no crypto-related products or services were disclosed. — RF Capital Q3 2024 Report to Shareholders

Financial news / other:

  • July 29, 2024, company press release: Richardson Wealth announced Marcus Chun as Head of Digital Strategies and Advisor Services, with responsibilities tied to technology alignment, process improvement, and advisor services rather than digital assets. — Richardson Wealth press release
  • March 25, 2022, SEDAR+ issuer profile: confirms the correct entity as RF Capital Group Inc., formerly GMP Capital Inc., helping disambiguate the issuer used for the search. — SEDAR+ issuer profile

CME GROUP

  • Rank: 80 | Expo: 0.97 | id_str: US12572Q1058
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 1.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: earnings-call transcripts archive (2019–2024), CME Group annual reports / 10-K filings (2017, 2023), company crypto product press releases (2017, 2021), monthly volume disclosures (2024)

Firm Overview

CME Group Inc. is a large U.S.-listed derivatives exchange operator whose core business is futures, options, clearing, market data, and related benchmark products across asset classes. Within that broader exchange franchise, its crypto exposure comes from directly listing and supporting regulated cryptocurrency derivatives and reference-rate products rather than from holding crypto on balance sheet or mining it.

Exposure Channels Identified

CME Group fits finserv because it directly offers crypto-linked financial products: bitcoin futures launched in December 2017, ether futures launched in February 2021, and the firm later expanded into micro contracts, options, event contracts, ratio futures, and additional cryptocurrency reference rates. The 2017 annual report explicitly states that management “seized the opportunity” to launch bitcoin futures, and the 2023 10-K lists Bitcoin futures, Micro Bitcoin and Ether options, euro-denominated Bitcoin and Ether futures, cryptocurrency reference rates, event contracts on Bitcoin futures, and Ether/Bitcoin Ratio futures as part of the company’s product slate.

This is not holders, because the retrieved filings and releases describe exchange-traded and benchmark products rather than treasury investment in crypto. It is not miners, bizserv, or infrastr, because the evidenced activity is the direct provision of regulated crypto market access and related benchmarks, which falls squarely under crypto financial services.

Weight Assignment

finserv receives 1.0 because the crypto linkage is explicit, repeated across management materials, and economically tied to CME’s role as a marketplace operator offering trading, clearing, and benchmark services for bitcoin- and ether-related contracts. The evidence does not support any balance-sheet crypto holding program, mining activity, or non-financial support-service role. invbase is 0 because, although CME is clearly active in crypto derivatives, the retrieved sources did not explicitly characterize CME stock itself as a crypto proxy or document crypto-driven shareholder-flow dynamics.

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main limitation is transcript accessibility: the Seeking Alpha archive clearly confirms multiple CME earnings-call transcripts across 2019–2024, but full transcript text was not directly retrievable from the accessible search results, so the transcript anchor is based on the archived transcript record together with company earnings materials and filings rather than a long directly scraped transcript passage. Revenue materiality is also only partially disclosed: crypto products are clearly part of CME’s offering and 2024 volume statistics show strong crypto contract activity, but the company does not appear in the retrieved filings to break out a separate crypto revenue line, so RevShare is coded conservatively as 1 rather than higher.

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • February 12, 2025, Seeking Alpha transcript archive: CME’s archive shows accessible earnings-call transcript listings for Q4 2024, Q3 2024, Q2 2024, Q1 2024, Q4 2023 and earlier periods, confirming sustained management discussion windows during the sample period. — Seeking Alpha transcript listing
  • February 12, 2025, Q4 2024 earnings conference-call materials: CME’s investor page for the Q4 2024 earnings call includes quarterly commentary and earnings-call materials, supporting use of company earnings documents for the latest sample-period anchor. — CME Group Q4 2024 earnings call page

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • 2017 annual report: “given increasing client interest in evolving cryptocurrency markets, we seized the opportunity to launch a bitcoin futures contract in 2017” — CME Group 2017 Annual Report
  • February 28, 2024, 2023 Form 10-K: CME listed “Bitcoin futures” among 2023 volume-record products and identified launches including “Micro Bitcoin and Ether Options,” “Euro-denominated Bitcoin and Ether Futures,” “Additional Cryptocurrency Reference Rates and Real-Time Indices,” “Event contracts on Bitcoin Futures,” and “Ether/Bitcoin Ratio Futures.” — CME Group 2023 Form 10-K
  • October 31, 2017, company press release: CME announced planned bitcoin futures, stating the contracts would be cash-settled and based on the CME CF Bitcoin Reference Rate. — CME Group announces launch of Bitcoin futures
  • February 8, 2021, company press release: CME stated it launched Ether futures, “further expanding its crypto derivatives offerings in this emerging asset class.” — CME Group announces launch of Ether futures
  • December 6, 2021, company press release: CME launched Micro Ether futures, “further expanding its suite of cryptocurrency derivatives offerings.” — CME Group announces launch of Micro Ether futures

Financial news / other:

  • January 3, 2025, company monthly volume release covering 2024: full-year 2024 cryptocurrency ADV increased 203% to 117,000 contracts ($6.8 billion notional), indicating crypto products were meaningful but still a small subset of the group’s much larger total exchange volume. — CME Group 2024 market statistics release
  • January 3, 2025, company press release: Q4 2024 cryptocurrency ADV increased 307% to 197,000 contracts, with December cryptocurrency ADV up 366% to 245,000 contracts, corroborating continuing crypto-market activity through the end of the sample horizon. — CME Group Q4/December 2024 volume highlights

NESTE

  • Rank: 81 | Expo: 0.95 | id_str: FI0009013296
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 1.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: NA
  • RevShare: NA | AssetShare: NA
  • Primary sources: company earnings materials (2024), annual report / financial statements, company sustainability news

Firm Overview

Neste Oyj is a Finnish energy company listed on Nasdaq Helsinki that focuses on renewable diesel, sustainable aviation fuel, and oil products. Across the retrieved 2024 investor materials and company disclosures, the firm is described as a renewable fuels and circular raw-materials producer rather than as a crypto-related operating business. (neste.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

No substantive crypto-exposure channel was evidenced in the retrieved sources for January 2017 to December 2024. The accessible 2024 earnings materials focus on renewable fuels margins, SAF volumes, refinery operations, feedstocks, and commodity and currency derivatives, with no discussion of cryptocurrency holdings, mining, exchanges, custody, payments, or crypto-linked customer services. (neste.com)

A 13 September 2022 company press release mentions Neste as one participant in a pilot using blockchain technology to improve supply-chain traceability and mass-balance certification. That evidence is not sufficient for bizserv or finserv, because Neste was not shown to be providing blockchain or crypto services commercially, holding digital assets, or deriving meaningful revenue from such activity; it was a sustainability-traceability pilot around certification processes. (neste.com)

Weight Assignment

unclass receives 1.0 because the retrieved evidence does not support any of the six substantive crypto-exposure mechanisms. The only blockchain-related item is an isolated supply-chain certification pilot, which falls below the project’s threshold for meaningful crypto exposure and does not demonstrate direct asset, revenue, infrastructure, or investor-base linkage to cryptocurrency. (neste.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main limitation is transcript accessibility: a Seeking Alpha transcript listing for Neste was discoverable, but full transcript pages were not directly fetchable in the browsing environment. However, accessible company earnings releases for Q2 2024, Q3 2024, and FY 2024 provided management commentary, and none contained substantive crypto discussion. The 2022 blockchain pilot could appear crypto-adjacent at first glance, but under the stated exclusion rules it is too incidental and operationally remote from cryptocurrency markets to justify a non-zero mechanism weight. (seekingalpha.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • 24 October 2024, company interim report / earnings-call materials: “the overcapacity in renewable fuel markets is creating pressure on margins” — management commentary focused on renewable fuels, margins, and SAF rather than crypto activities. — Neste Q3 2024 Interim Report
  • 25 July 2024, company half-year report / earnings-call materials: “Our result in the second quarter reflects the significantly weaker renewables market and the Porvoo refinery major turnaround” — management discussion centered on renewables operations and market conditions, with no crypto linkage. — Neste Half-Year Report January–June 2024
  • Seeking Alpha transcript archive listing: the archive shows Neste earnings-transcript availability, but full transcript text was not directly retrievable in the browsing environment. — Seeking Alpha transcript listing

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • 13 February 2025, Financial Statements Release 2024: management described 2024 as driven by renewable fuels, refinery performance, and commodity/currency derivatives; no cryptocurrency holdings or crypto business line were disclosed. — Neste Financial Statements Release 2024
  • 28 February 2025, Annual Report 2024 publication notice: the annual report is presented as covering annual review, governance, remuneration, board review, and financial statements, with no indicated crypto business activity. — Neste has published its Annual Report 2024

Financial news / other:

  • 13 September 2022, company press release: Neste participated in a pilot testing blockchain as a supplement to ISCC Plus mass-balance certification for supply-chain traceability; this indicates isolated technology experimentation, not substantive crypto exposure. — ISCC and Circularise pilot with Neste

LEONTEQ

  • Rank: 82 | Expo: 0.95 | id_str: CH0190891181
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 1.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: annual report 2024, half-year/full-year 2021 results materials, company crypto-product announcement (2021)

Firm Overview

Leonteq AG is a Zurich-based Swiss listed financial services firm focused on structured investment solutions, derivative products, and white-label issuance platforms for banks and other financial institutions. Within that core structured-products business, the retrieved sources show that Leonteq developed and marketed securitised products with crypto assets as underlyings during 2017–2024.

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports finserv exposure because Leonteq directly issues and structures crypto-linked investment products for institutional and retail investors rather than merely providing software or consulting to the ecosystem. Its 2024 annual report states that crypto assets are among its common structured-product underlyings, that it offers 37 crypto assets and crypto-asset indices, and that it purchases or sells the respective underlyings to hedge certificates it issues. (leonteq.com)

The evidence does not support holders, because the annual report explicitly frames the crypto assets on balance sheet as economic hedges for issued structured products rather than as a treasury investment position. It does not support miners, bizserv, or infrastr, because the retrieved materials show no mining operations, no non-financial crypto service line, and no crypto-dedicated hardware/hosting/energy infrastructure business. (leonteq.com)

Weight Assignment

finserv receives 1.0 because the crypto linkage is clearly embedded in Leonteq’s financial-services model: issuing tracker certificates and other structured products on crypto underlyings, distributing them to clients, and holding corresponding crypto inventory for hedging. There is no persuasive evidence of a separate treasury-HODL channel or a meaningful second mechanism that would justify splitting the weight, so all mechanism weight is assigned to finserv. (leonteq.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

A full earnings-call transcript for 2017–2024 was not directly retrievable in the browsing environment from Seeking Alpha or company IR pages, so the transcript anchor is approximated with company results materials and management-style investor communications rather than a clean transcript page; certainty is therefore capped below the maximum. Revenue materiality is also only partially disclosed: Leonteq gave explicit crypto-underlying revenue figures for 2021, but comparable crypto-specific revenue segmentation was not found for every later year, so RevShare is based on the clearest disclosed period rather than a continuous annual series. (leonteq.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • 22 July 2021, half-year results / management results materials: “Revenues from products with crypto assets as underlyings grew to CHF 9.2 million” — management results statement shows crypto-linked product revenue as part of Leonteq’s operating business. — Leonteq publishes half-year 2021 results
  • 2021 half-year report, management commentary: Leonteq stated that it expanded “our product offering for crypto assets as underlyings,” indicating crypto products were part of the business mix discussed in investor reporting. — Leonteq Half-Year Report 2021
  • 2024 annual report / investor materials: “Investing in crypto assets via Leonteq structured products provides several advantages compared to direct investments” — investor-facing management materials present crypto products as an active structured-product offering. — Leonteq Annual Report 2024

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • 2024 annual report: “With 37 different crypto assets available, Leonteq offers one of the largest universes that can be securitised” — direct disclosure of the breadth of Leonteq’s crypto-product universe. — Leonteq Annual Report 2024
  • 2024 annual report, note on trading inventories: Leonteq disclosed that it issues certificates on single crypto assets and thematic crypto baskets and buys or sells the respective underlyings to hedge those certificates. — Leonteq Annual Report 2024
  • 22 July 2021, half-year 2021 results: crypto-underlying product revenues were CHF 9.2 million in H1 2021, described as a fifteen-fold increase year on year. — Leonteq H1 2021 Press Release PDF
  • 10 February 2022, full-year 2021 results: Leonteq disclosed that revenues from products with crypto assets as underlyings reached CHF 17.4 million in 2021, a sixfold increase from 2020. — Leonteq publishes full-year 2021 results

Financial news / other:

  • 12 August 2021, company announcement: Leonteq launched its crypto-asset offering in Germany and Austria, offering structured products on 18 crypto assets to institutional and retail investors and describing itself as a pioneer in crypto structured products since 2017/2018. — Leonteq launches its crypto asset offering in Germany and Austria
  • 3 October 2017, Structured Retail Products: Leonteq launched exchange-traded notes tracking bitcoin for investors seeking bitcoin exposure, corroborating early-period crypto-product activity. — Leonteq moves to capitalise on the 'rapid rise' of bitcoin

XINYUAN REAL ESTATE ADR 1:20

  • Rank: 83 | Expo: 0.94 | id_str: US98417P2048
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 0.0, bizserv 1.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 0 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: earnings-call transcripts (Q4 2018, Q4 2019), company press releases on blockchain strategy, Form 20-F filings

Firm Overview

Xinyuan Real Estate Co., Ltd. is a China-focused residential real estate developer listed in the United States through ADSs, with additional property activity in the United States. Across the 2017–2024 sample, its core reported business remained property development, but management and company disclosures also described a blockchain-technology initiative tied to real-estate-related technology services rather than direct crypto financial intermediation. (fool.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports bizserv rather than finserv or holders. On the Q4 2018 earnings call, when asked about blockchain initiatives, management said the “application for blockchain technology in Xinyuan is now in the very early phase of exploration,” especially in combination with its traditional real estate business, which indicates a technology/service initiative rather than treasury crypto holdings, mining, or a crypto exchange/brokerage business. (fool.com)

Company communications also framed Xinyuan as pursuing blockchain technology services. A 2017 company release said it had become “one of the first blockchain technology service providers in China,” while another company release described a technology initiative with blockchain platform developer Ruizhuoxitou. However, the retrieved 2024 half-year results still described the company primarily as a real estate developer, and the available 20-F materials did not show bitcoin holdings, mining revenue, custody/trading activity, or crypto-backed product issuance. (ir.xyre.com)

Weight Assignment

bizserv receives 1.0 because the crypto-adjacent activity evidenced in the transcript and press materials is a blockchain technology/service initiative connected to real estate applications, not direct handling of digital assets. There is no persuasive evidence for holders, miners, finserv, or infrastr: the available SEC filings and results materials do not disclose balance-sheet crypto, mining operations, exchange/custody/payment activity, or crypto-dedicated hardware/hosting infrastructure. (fool.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main limitation is that the blockchain disclosures are stronger in company press releases and a transcript Q&A than in later financial filings; the accessible 20-F pages retrieved in this search did not provide a clearly quantified blockchain revenue line. In addition, some investor-relations transcript and press-release pages were difficult to fetch directly in the browsing environment, so the classification relies on the retrievable transcript text plus search-result snippets and SEC filings. That keeps certainty below the maximum and suggests the blockchain activity was likely peripheral to the core property business. (fool.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • 15 February 2019, Q4 2018 earnings call transcript: “the application for blockchain technology in Xinyuan is now in the very early phase of exploration” — management said blockchain was being explored for use with Xinyuan’s traditional real-estate business. — The Motley Fool transcript
  • 3 April 2020, Q4 2019 earnings call availability: the company IR archive shows a dedicated transcript for Q4 2019, corroborating ongoing investor discussion during the sample even though the fetchable text in this environment was limited. — Xinyuan Quarterly Results archive

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • 2024 first-half results release: Xinyuan described itself as an NYSE-listed real estate developer operating primarily in China with U.S. projects, with no crypto revenue line or digital-asset operating disclosure in the release. — SEC Exhibit 99.1, October 25 2024
  • 2024 Form 20-F: the annual report discusses property development, debt, and technology-service acquisitions, but the retrieved filing text did not show disclosed bitcoin, digital-currency, blockchain-token, or mining positions. — SEC Form 20-F for 2024
  • 2020 Form 20-F: search within the filing returned no matches for “blockchain,” “bitcoin,” “digital currency,” or “token,” indicating no prominent crypto-specific balance-sheet or business-line disclosure in that annual report. — SEC Form 20-F for 2019 filed April 29 2020

Financial news / other:

  • 2018 company release: Xinyuan said it had become “one of the first blockchain technology service providers in China,” directly characterizing the exposure as technology services. — Xinyuan Speeds up Real Estate Innovation with Blockchain
  • 2017 company release: Xinyuan announced an update on its technology initiative with blockchain platform developer Ruizhuoxitou, supporting a blockchain-service orientation rather than a crypto-financial one. — Xinyuan Real Estate Co., Ltd. Announces Update on its Technology Strategy

SIGNATURE BANK

  • Rank: 84 | Expo: 0.92 | id_str: US82669G1040
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 1.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: earnings call transcripts (2021–2022), FDIC post-failure review, 2022 Form 10-K release / company disclosures

Firm Overview

Signature Bank was a New York-based commercial bank listed in the United States as SBNY, with core activities in private-client and commercial banking. Across the 2017–2024 sample, the retrieved evidence shows that crypto exposure came through banking and payments services for digital-asset clients, especially its Signet blockchain-based payments platform, rather than through proprietary crypto holdings or mining. (businesswire.com)

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports finserv only. In earnings calls, management repeatedly described a digital-asset banking business serving exchanges and other crypto clients, and presented Signet as a blockchain-based payments product embedded in the bank’s service offering. On the Q1 2022 call, management said the bank had onboarded a record number of digital-asset clients and that major exchanges were integrating with Signet, while the Q2 2022 and Q4 2022 calls discussed declines in digital-asset deposits and identified the digital ecosystem as the largest on Signet. (fool.com)

There is no persuasive evidence for holders, miners, bizserv, or infrastr. The retrieved regulatory and filing-related sources characterize Signature as a bank serving digital-asset companies, with deposits from that client base and no loans to digital-asset firms at year-end 2022; they do not indicate treasury crypto on the balance sheet, mining activity, or sale of crypto-focused hardware/hosting infrastructure. (fdic.gov)

Weight Assignment

finserv receives 1.0 because the crypto linkage was direct financial intermediation: deposit gathering from digital-asset firms and operation of a real-time blockchain-based payments network for commercial clients. The activity was repeatedly discussed by management, was economically meaningful enough to affect deposit trends and market perception, and fits the project’s financial-services definition much more closely than any service, infrastructure, or holder category. (fool.com)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main limitation is that the bank failed in March 2023, so some primary investor-relations materials are no longer easily retrievable directly, and the browsing environment could not fetch the Seeking Alpha transcript archive. However, multiple Motley Fool transcript pages were accessible, and post-failure FDIC and other regulatory materials provide unusually strong corroboration. RevShare is kept at 1 rather than higher because the evidence points to meaningful but still minority deposit/payment exposure rather than a disclosed crypto-dominated revenue segment; AssetShare is 0 because the retrieved sources indicate no proprietary crypto asset holdings. (fool.com)

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • 19 April 2022, Q1 2022 earnings call transcript: “On the digital front, we are seeing tremendous positive momentum”; management added that the bank onboarded a record 160 new clients and that major exchanges were integrating with Signet. — The Motley Fool transcript (fool.com)
  • 19 July 2022, Q2 2022 earnings call transcript: management said the “digital asset banking team” declined by $2.4 billion in deposits, showing direct exposure through crypto-linked banking balances. — The Motley Fool transcript (fool.com)
  • 19 October 2021, Q3 2021 earnings call transcript: management discussed the “digital asset business” and said banks entering the space were trying to use a “Signet type product,” underscoring the bank’s blockchain-payments service orientation. — The Motley Fool transcript (fool.com)
  • 17 January 2023, Q4 2022 earnings call transcript: an analyst referred to the “digital ecosystem” as “the largest on Signet,” corroborating crypto as the main Signet use case. — The Motley Fool transcript (fool.com)

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • 2 March 2023, 2022 Form 10-K release: Signature said it was “the first FDIC-insured bank to launch a blockchain-based digital payments platform,” and described Signet as allowing commercial clients to make real-time U.S. dollar payments. — Business Wire 2022 Form 10-K release (businesswire.com)
  • 2023 FDIC material on Signature failure: the FDIC stated that as of year-end 2022, deposits related to digital-asset companies were about 20% of total deposits and that the bank had no loans to digital-asset firms. — FDIC speech on recent bank failures (fdic.gov)
  • 28 April 2023, FDIC Office of Inspector General review: the report said digital-asset deposits increased substantially in 2020–2021 and that digital-asset-related deposits represented 62% of the bank’s 2022 deposit outflow. — FDIC’s Supervision of Signature Bank (PDF) (fdic.gov)

Financial news / other:

  • 12 March 2023, Axios: Signature was described as “a key bank to the cryptocurrency industry” and “one of the most crypto-friendly institutions on Wall Street,” supporting invbase = 1 and market treatment as a crypto proxy. — Axios (axios.com)
  • 10 March 2023, CNBC: the bank’s shares fell as investors worried that “any crypto-related bank could be in danger,” indicating equity-market sentiment spillovers tied directly to crypto exposure. — CNBC (cnbc.com)

BLACK HILLS

  • Rank: 85 | Expo: 0.90 | id_str: US0921131092
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 0.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 1.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 0
  • Certainty: 3
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 0
  • Primary sources: 2023 Form 10-K / annual report, 2019 and 2021 earnings call transcripts, 2024 investor-relations earnings release

Firm Overview

Black Hills Corp. is a U.S. regulated electric and natural gas utility headquartered in South Dakota and listed on the NYSE under BKH. Within the 2017–2024 sample period, the firm’s crypto linkage appears not through holding or transacting in digital assets, but through supplying electric transmission and tariff-based utility service to blockchain and crypto-mining customers in Wyoming.

Exposure Channels Identified

The evidence supports infrastr because management repeatedly described blockchain and crypto miners as incremental electric-load customers served through Wyoming Electric’s system and through a dedicated Blockchain Interruptible Service tariff. In the 2023 annual report, Black Hills said it had “successfully served expanding data center and blockchain energy demand” and highlighted “unique tariffs to serve data centers and large blockchain customers.” (sec.gov)

Earnings calls reinforce that this was infrastructure exposure rather than financial or treasury exposure. In May 2019, management said the tariff would “help recruit blockchain companies” and characterized blockchain load as new electricity demand tied to “cryptocurrencies” and “blockchain mining” in Wyoming. In November 2021, management said the Ready Wyoming transmission project would better serve “data centers and blockchain and crypto miners,” and that the company had received numerous requests to serve crypto-mining businesses. (fool.com)

No evidence was found that Black Hills held crypto on its balance sheet, mined bitcoin itself, operated exchanges/custody/payment platforms, or provided non-financial advisory/software services to crypto firms. The 2023 Form 10-K/annual report contains blockchain-customer and tariff disclosures but no references to bitcoin or crypto assets held by the company. (sec.gov)

Weight Assignment

infrastr receives 1.0 because the identified mechanism is straightforwardly utility and transmission infrastructure sold to blockchain/crypto-mining customers. The activity is described as load growth served through Black Hills’ electric system and specialized tariff structures, which fits the project’s infrastructure definition more closely than bizserv; there is no evidence supporting holders, miners, or finserv. Because the disclosures suggest a niche but real growth avenue rather than a core corporate transformation, RevShare is set to 1 and AssetShare to 0. (sec.gov)

Ambiguities / Limitations

The main limitation is that some investor-relations pages and transcript pages were only partially accessible in the browsing environment, and the CoinDesk article about the 2022 Wyoming bitcoin-mining power deal could not be directly fetched due to access restrictions. However, the accessible Motley Fool transcripts and the company’s 2023 annual report provide direct management-language evidence sufficient to anchor classification. invbase remains 0 because the retrieved materials show operating exposure to crypto-related customers, but not explicit evidence that Black Hills’ shareholder base or equity trading was treated as a crypto proxy. ()

Evidence

Earnings calls:

  • 3 May 2019, Q1 2019 earnings call transcript: “The tariff will help recruit blockchain companies to call Wyoming their home, delivering additional energy demand to our system.” — The Motley Fool transcript (fool.com)
  • 3 May 2019, Q1 2019 earnings call transcript: CFO said Wyoming legislation was advantageous for “your cryptocurrencies and your blockchain mining kind of activities” and that Black Hills was speaking with potential blockchain customers. — The Motley Fool transcript (fool.com)
  • 3 November 2021, Q3 2021 earnings call transcript: management said the Ready Wyoming project would better serve “data centers and blockchain and crypto miners.” — The Motley Fool transcript (fool.com)
  • 3 November 2021, Q3 2021 earnings call transcript: management said it received “numerous request[s] to serve crypto mining businesses” and had a blockchain interruptible service tariff in place. — The Motley Fool transcript (fool.com)

Filings (10-K / press releases):

  • 14 February 2024, 2023 Form 10-K / annual report: Black Hills said it “successfully served expanding data center and blockchain energy demand” and had “unique tariffs to serve data centers and large blockchain customers.” — SEC-filed 2023 Annual Report PDF (sec.gov)
  • 14 February 2024, 2023 Form 10-K / annual report: glossary defines the “Blockchain Interruptible Service (BCIS) Tariff” for prospective Wyoming Electric blockchain customers of 10 MW or greater. — SEC-filed 2023 Annual Report PDF (sec.gov)
  • 14 February 2024, 2023 Form 10-K / annual report: Black Hills said Wyoming Electric completed its first BCIS agreement in June 2022 for up to 45 MW, with expansion to 75 MW exercised in 2023. — SEC-filed 2023 Annual Report PDF (sec.gov)

Financial news / other:

  • 21 June 2022, CoinDesk search result summary: Black Hills was described as signing a deal through its Wyoming subsidiary to provide power to a bitcoin-mining operation in Cheyenne for at least five years. Direct article retrieval was blocked, so this is corroborative rather than primary evidence. — CoinDesk (coindesk.com)
  • 6 November 2024, company earnings-release search snippet: Black Hills said its “innovative tariffs” were advancing its “data center and blockchain growth strategy,” expecting these customers to rise from 5% of EPS in 2023 to 10% or more by 2028. — Black Hills investor relations release (ir.blackhillscorp.com)

MONEYGRAM INTL. DEAD - DELIST.02/06/23

  • Rank: 86 | Expo: 0.90 | id_str: US60935Y2081
  • Weight summary: holders 0.0, miners 0.0, finserv 1.0, bizserv 0.0, infrastr 0.0, unclass 0.0
  • Investor base amplifier (invbase): 1
  • Certainty: 4
  • RevShare: 1 | AssetShare: 1
  • Primary sources: earnings call transcripts (2019–2021), SEC filings (2019–2021 10-K/10-Q), financial news on the Ripple partnership

Firm Overview

MoneyGram International, Inc. was a U.S.-listed cross-border money transfer and payments company until it was taken private in 2023.
